        If an image is available, include visual judgment.

        2. **extracted_insight**
           - Provide a concrete scientific observation, not generic praise.

        3. **suggested_keywords**

            Metadata: {"metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\", \"figure_id\": \"img_71\", \"figure_title\": \"Fig. 4. Estimated coefficients (date) prior to and following the start time of heating.\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden\\\\figure_4.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Results & Findings\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Present Quantitative Results\", \"Compare Experimental Performance\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Quantitative Charts\", \"sub_types\": [\"Bar Chart\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"AQI\", \"PM2.5\", \"SO2\", \"NO2\", \"Eastern region\", \"Western region\", \"WCHP project\"], \"concise_summary\": \"The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.\", \"references\": [\"As Fig. 4 shows, the 15 day estimator prior to and following the start of heating varied around a value of zero (0) devoid of a clear trend, and most coefficients were not statistically significant. This result indicated a similarity between pilot cities and non-pilot cities regarding AQI. Despite an abnormal increase 4-6 days following the start time of heating, such differences decreased to a negative value (as seen 2 days prior to the start time of heating), and this trend maintained for the ensuing two weeks. These results were consistent with the hypothesis that the WCHP project generated differential trends in pollutant concentrations between pilot and non-pilot cities. It is noteworthy that the period prior to the start time of heating was implemented and continued for 3-4 days in some cities prior to the formal start time of heating, which may have resulted in the early decrease. Additionally, we also estimated the Heatperiod coefficient 30 days following the start time of heating, which also indicated that effects of the WCHP project existed following the start time of heating.\", \"where  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  is the set of dummy variables, which represents whether the  $i$ -th city is in a heating period at  $n-1$ -day.  $L_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days prior to the start time of heating, and  $E_n$  represents a series of estimated coefficients 15 days following the start time of heating. With the omitted time category being the start time of heating,  $L_n$  captures the differences in pollutant indicators between pilot and non-pilot cities relative to differences just prior to the launch of the policy. Here we selected AQI as the air pollutant proxy. Under such specifications, if the signs of the estimated  $Heatperiod_{i,n-1}$  coefficients differ, it can be assumed that changes in pollutant concentrations are not significant.\", \"As a second robustness check, we constructed a counterfactual test to check whether our results could be driven by other unobserved factors. In doing so, we selected a different date during the non-heating period (May, June, July, August, September, and October) in lieu of the real start date of heating, specifying the interaction term between the treatment variable and the pseudoheating start time variable as Pseudo-HCP. The WCHP project can only take effect during the heating period, while the pseudo-HCP coefficient can reflect the impact of the WCHP project during the non-heating period. If estimated coefficients of pseudo-WCHP are significant and have the same sign as of the true WCHP shown in Table 4, we can conclude that air quality improvements in pilot cities could not be mainly attributed to the WCHP project, and the robustness of the DID model would be inadequate. In this case, improvements may be related to other pollution control policies, such as production restrictions and limits to the number of private vehicle allowed on roads.\", \"According to the regressions shown in Table 5, only when the start time of heating was specified in October, coefficients of pseudo-WCHP were significantly negative at a  $1\\\\%$  level, while other estimation results were not significant or had a positive sign. Compared to Table 4, these conflicting results failed to reject the validity of the \\\"WHCD\\\" policy. That is to say, the effect of the WCHP project was evident. In fact, we specified the heating start time on the 15th of November in consideration of the \\\"official\\\" start time of central heating while ignoring differences caused by scattered coal combustion. In October, some rural areas in the pilot and non-pilot cities began to burn scattered coal for heating, which could cause an evident increase in pollution emissions [47,48].\"], \"chunk_type\": \"figure\"}", "chunkid": "chunk_004515", "paperid": "paper_016", "type": "picture", "paper_id": "paper_016"}

The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas."}

(For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\\\figure_1.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Data & Experimental Setup\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Introduce Experimental Setup\", \"Describe Data Distribution\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Geospatial Visualizations\", \"sub_types\": [\"Map\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"CONUS\", \"ozonesonde stations\", \"CMAQ\", \"WRF\", \"urban regions\", \"rural regions\", \"Boulder\", \"Huntsville\"], \"concise_summary\": \"The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville).\", \"inferred_insight\": \"Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.\", \"references\": [\"To compute both atmospheric concentrations of gaseous and particulate pollutants and first-order sensitivity coefficients, required for conducting inverse modeling, we use the Community Multiscale Air Quality model configured with the decoupled direct method in three dimensions (CMAQ DDM-3D) based on CMAQ model version 5.2 (Cohan et al., 2005; Napelenok et al., 2006), developed and released by the EPA. Our study domain covering the CONUS utilizes  $12\\\\mathrm{km}$  grid spacing with  $459\\\\times 299$  grid points (shown in Fig. 1), while the vertical extent between the surface and  $100\\\\mathrm{hPa}$  is discretized with 27 layers of variable thickness. We use carbon bond (CB6r3) for the gas phase and AERO6 for the aerosol chemical mechanism. We obtained the chemical initial and boundary conditions from the CMAQ model with the in-line dust model covering the entire Northern Hemisphere (HCMAQ) (Mathur et al., 2017), providing dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions in a consistent physical configuration. Details of the HCMAQ simulation and its emission data can be found in Jung et al. (2021) and Vukovich et al. (2018). Since the HCMAQ simulation was based on the year 2016, we applied seasonal scaling for gaseous species based on the comparison\", \"Using the Weather Research and Forecasting (WRF) model version 4.0, we estimated meteorological fields over the U.S.  $12\\\\mathrm{km}$  modeling domain (shown in Fig. 1) with the initial and boundary conditions from the National Centers for Environmental Prediction (NCEP) North American Regional Reanalysis (NARR), which has a  $32\\\\mathrm{km}$  horizontal resolution and  $3\\\\mathrm{h}$  of temporal resolution. Detailed configurations of both the CMAQ and WRF models can be found in Table 1. In an effort to enhance model performance in simulating meteorological fields, we applied indirect soil moisture and temperature nudging technique (Pleim and Gilliam, 2009; Pleim and Xiu, 2003) together with a four-\", \"The variability of satellite-observed  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO columns reflects changes in bottom-up emissions and the contribution of background sources in the upper troposphere (Silvern et al., 2019). In Fig. 3, we plotted seasonal averaged changes in observed and simulated tropospheric  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column densities in four regions: domainwide (i.e., CONUS), urban and rural regions, and southeastern (SE) areas. We defined an urban area based on the U.S. census-based surrogate, where population density is at least 1000 people per square mile, denoted in red in Fig. 1; and we defined a rural region, designated by gray in Fig. 1, as more than  $20\\\\mathrm{km}$  away from both urban regions and power plants with a capacity of 1 MW or more, based on the U.S. Energy Information Administration. The southeastern (SE) area (a blue rectangle shown in Fig. 1) denotes an area with dominant lightning-induced (to the total  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions) and biogenic emissions (to the total NMVOCs emissions) over the CONUS. In SE, NMVOCs emissions from biogenic sources are dominant, so we excluded the SE area when we analyzed modeled and observed HCHO columns over urban and rural regions in Fig. 3.\", \"Fig. 3. The long-term trend in summertime averaged tropospheric (a-1 to h-1)  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and (a-2 to h-2) HCHO columns observed by the OMI satellite (2011-2018) and simulated by the CMAQ model (2011, 2014, and 2017), and their relative changes in the CONUS and urban, rural, and southeastern U.S. regions (shown in Fig. 1). CMAQ simulations were conducted with the prior emission and three background setups (with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and lightning emissions (LNO), with dynamic lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions, and with profile lateral boundary conditions and without lightning emissions).\"], \"chunk_type\": \"figure\"}", "type": "picture", "paper_id": "paper_001"}
            Content: {"text": "The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville).             --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_002662
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"chunkid": "chunk_002662", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"HTAP_v2.2: a mosaic of regional and global emission grid maps for 2008 and 2010 to study hemispheric transport of air pollution\", \"md_filename\": \"HTAP_v2.2_ a mosaic of regional and global emissio.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 21, \"content_length\": 798, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "type": "texture", "paperid": "paper_011", "paper_id": "paper_011"}
            Content: {"text": "The resulting data set, named HTAP_v2.2, is a compilation of annual and monthly grid maps of anthropogenic air pollution emissions (with a  $0.1^{\\circ} \\times 0.1^{\\circ}$  grid resolution). It contains region-specific information on human activity (concerning intensity and geospatial distribution) and on fuel, technology- and process-dependent emission factors and end-of-pipe abatement, but it is not as consistent as a globally consistent emission inventory using international statistics and global geospatial distributions. With the perspective of being used in chemical transport models, this inventory includes the atmospheric gaseous pollutants  $\\mathrm{(SO_2, NO_x, CO, NMVOC^4, NH_3)}$  and particulate matter with carbonaceous speciation  $\\mathrm{(PM_{10}, PM_{2.5}, BC}$  and OC)5."}

            ID: chunk_000814

            Content: {"text": "The aerosol model and assimilation system with which the real time MACC aerosol analyses and forecasts are produced since July 2008 (Morcette et al., 2009; Benedetti et al., 2009) is being used to link the smoke emissions to atmospheric aerosol observations. Dust and sea salt are represented in three size bins each. Organic matter (OM) and black carbon are (BC) represented as two types (hydrophilic and hydrophobic) each.             Content: {"text": "A harmonized global, gridded, air pollution emission data set has been compiled with officially reported, gridded inventories at the national scale, to the extent possible and complemented with science-based inventories for regions and sectors where nationally reported data were not available."}

Hourly average on-road vehicle emission intensities of the  10\\\\%  high emitters under different congestion scenarios during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time).\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Quantifying on-road vehicle emissions during traff\\\\figure_4.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Results & Findings\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Present Core Findings\", \"Showcase Prediction Results\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Geospatial Visualizations\", \"sub_types\": [\"Map\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"vehicle emissions\", \"rush hours\", \"high emitters\", \"NOx\", \"HC\", \"CO\", \"congestion scenarios\"], \"concise_summary\": \"Spatial maps illustrate hourly average emissions (NOx, HC, CO) of the top 10% vehicle emitters under varying congestion scenarios in rush hours.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"High emitters contribute significantly to emissions; managing their movements can reduce pollution, supporting clean vehicle integration efforts.\", \"references\": [\"On this basis, the spatial distributions of vehicle emissions from the  $10\\\\%$  high emitters were depicted in detail (Fig. 4, Figs. S5-S6). Scenarios M1-5 and M6-10 represented vehicle emissions during rush hours and off-peak hours, respectively, with the accumulated mileages of the  $10\\\\%$  high emitters corresponding to M1-M5 in Table 1. It was found that the emissions of the target high emitters decreased sharply from M1 to M5, or M6 to M10. Thus, strict restrictions on the travels of these high emitters can effectively reduce road vehicle emissions, implying that accelerating clean substitution of these high emitters will be a feasible approach. As shown in Fig. S7, pre-China 3 vehicles made up around  $50\\\\%$  of these  $10\\\\%$  high emitters. We designed four different clean alternative scenarios\", \"This study aims to study the current status of vehicle emissions in China from a big data perspective. A newly updated dataset of 159,051 light-duty gasoline vehicles from Xiaoshan District, Hangzhou, were used, believed to be the best available sample of real emissions to conduct an analysis. Vehicle-specific emission factors were calculated on the basis of the high-resolution big data of vehicle emission measurements. As a result, the high contributions from target high emitters and significant differences among different manufacturers were quantified. Real-time online traffic monitoring big data in Xiaoshan District provide strong support to quantify road vehicle exhaust emissions. Consequently, the hyper-fine maps of on-road vehicle emissions were obtained. Thus, environmental effects of traffic congestion and to what extent it will offset the benefits of emission standards upgrading were further illustrated. Finally, practical approaches to vehicle emission controls under realistic conditions were proposed, providing new insights for urban vehicle emission management.\", \"Xiaoshan District is at the forefront of the country in the digital transformation and upgrading of the government. It is one of the few areas that have achieved full coverage of integrated traffic monitoring, and has been interconnected through its intelligent transportation system (called \\\"city brain\\\") since 2017 (Hua, 2018). This provide the opportunity to obtain detailed road traffic and road network information. According to 2021 China Urban Transportation Report released by Baidu Map (Baidu Map, 2021), the congestion index during rush hours of Hangzhou ranks top 9 among cities with  $>3$  million motor vehicles. As the transportation hub of Hangzhou, Xiaoshan District, with dense road network, its speed and traffic flow distribution characteristics can well represent the traffic conditions of large cities. Equipped with real-time online traffic monitoring facilities, the core road network in Xiaoshan District is divided into 4585 road segments. Traffic video records, together with image recognition algorithms (Redmon et al., 2016), were applied to detect vehicle license plates and thus monitor traffic fluxes, vehicle categories and driving speeds. These license plate recognition (LPR) data include the records of traffic fluxes and vehicle-specific speeds of on-road vehicles in Xiaoshan District. The average daily detection records were 4.23 million. As a result, we constructed a dataset of total  $1.53 \\\\times 10^{9}$  records from 1 January 2021 to 31 December 2021.\", \"from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time) and off-peak hours (from 9:00 to 16:30 and from 18:30 to 7:00 local time), respectively. On this basis, two smooth traffic (all speeds  $>25\\\\mathrm{km / h}$ ) scenarios (S1 and S4) and two severe congested scenarios (S3 and S6) were designed to represent different traffic conditions. Fleet composition scenario F0 was designed based on the local official vehicle Inspect/Maintenance (I/M) dataset, representing the current composition of road vehicles. On this basis, F1 and F2 scenarios were designed by considering the upgrading of emission standards and the increasing proportion of electric vehicles, respectively. Combining traffic congestion scenarios S1-S6 and fleet composition scenarios F0-F3, a total of 18 scenarios were studied (Table 2 and Table 3). For example, scenario S2F0 quantified the vehicle emissions when the traffic condition is S2 and the fleet composition is F0, which represents the current rush-hour traffic condition and current on-road fleet compositions. Similarly, scenario S5F0 quantified the vehicle emissions when the traffic condition is S5 and the fleet composition is F0, which represents the current traffic condition during off-peak hours and current on-road fleet compositions. In the subsequent comparison, scenarios S2F0 and S5F0 were used as the benchmark scenarios for rush hour and off-peak hours, respectively. Overall, our results show that under the ideal situations of no traffic congestion (i.e., S1 and S4), the emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  will be significantly reduced with the continuous upgrading of fleet composition (from F0 to F2). However, the impacts of traffic\", \"Fig. 6. (a) Vehicle emission factors under different driving speeds. (b) Spatial distribution of hourly average on-road vehicle driving speeds (in km/h) during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time). (c) Spatial distribution of hourly average on-road vehicle driving speeds (in km/h) during off-peak hours (from 9:00 to 16:30 and from 18:30 to 7:00 local time). (d) Spatial distribution of hourly average traffic fluxes during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time). (e) Spatial distribution of hourly average traffic fluxes during off-peak hours (from 9:00 to 16:30 and from 18:30 to 7:00 local time).\", \"Table 2 Scenarios during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time) and their relative percentage changes in on-road vehicle emissions compared to S2F0.\", \"standard upgrading, and even increased on-road vehicle emissions instead (see S1F2, S2F2, S3F2 in Table 2). Similarly, if the traffic congestion were effectively alleviated (S4F0) during the off-peak hours, the hourly emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  could be reduced by  $-14.2\\\\%$ ,  $-21.4\\\\%$ , and  $-9.6\\\\%$  respectively, but increased by  $7.8\\\\%$ ,  $11.4\\\\%$ , and  $5.2\\\\%$  if the congestions were aggravated (S6F0) (Figs. S11-S13, Table 3). Note that, in the southern areas of the district, the emission intensities in the arterial and residential streets far exceeded ( $\\\\sim 5$  times) those in the northern areas (Fig. 7, Fig. S11). These high emission hotspots were mainly attributed to the high traffic fluxes and low traffic speeds. Frequent traffic congestion, accelerations and decelerations led to significant increases in vehicles' fuel consumption. Thus, better traffic management policies should be applied to reduce vehicles' energy consumption and mitigate pollutant emissions as well (Huang et al., 2020b).\", \"Based on the newly updated big data of high-resolution vehicle emission profiles, the emission characteristics of light gasoline vehicles were analyzed in this study. The extent to which pollutant emission factors varied with different emission standards, vehicle manufactures and operating modes were illustrated in detail. The significant influence of traffic congestion on vehicle emissions was addressed with the support of the real-time on-road traffic monitoring system in Xiaoshan District. Additionally, several approaches to reducing on-road vehicle emissions under realistic conditions were proposed from a big data perspective. The results showed that the emissions of LDGVs in China have decreased significantly (75.7–86.2 %) with the continuous upgrading of emission standards (i.e., from China 2 to China 5) in the past 13 years. Heavy-tailed distributions were found in CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  emission factors. The results showed that the  $10\\\\%$  high emitters contributed  $>60\\\\%$  to the total fleet emissions, significantly higher than previous studies. In order to achieve greater emission reductions in the future, it is necessary to strengthen the gradual replacements of pre-China 3 vehicles, especially those in the  $10\\\\%$  high emitters. Large differences were found in the emissions of vehicles produced by different manufacturers. It was found that the emission factors of the cleanest and most polluted vehicles produced by different manufacturers differed by 33–49 times. Therefore, in the future, the supervision of\", \"Alobaidi, M.K., Badri, R.M., Salman, M.M., 2020. Evaluating the negative impact of traffic congestion on air pollution at signalized intersection.             Metadata: {"chunkid": "chunk_000485", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Air quality changes in China 2013-2020: Effectiveness of clean coal technology policies\", \"md_filename\": \"Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 41, \"content_length\": 550, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "type": "texture", "paperid": "paper_031", "paper_id": "paper_031"}
            Content: {"text": "Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/)."}

        # Role
        You are an **Evaluator** in a scientific literature investigation team.
        Your task is to evaluate each retrieved evidence item independently and provide structured feedback for planning.

        # Evaluation Logic
        Read title, summary, and insight carefully.
        If an image is available, include visual judgment.

        1. **branch_action**
           - **GROW**: high-value evidence, strongly relevant, with clear follow-up clues.
           - **KEEP**: medium-value evidence, relevant but limited expansion value.
           - **PRUNE**: low-value evidence, irrelevant/noisy/redundant.

        2. **extracted_insight**
           - Provide a concrete scientific observation, not generic praise.

        3. **suggested_keywords**

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_004609
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\", \"figure_id\": \"img_165\", \"figure_title\": \"Figure 12. Grid maps for gaseous (a) and aerosol (b) species in the MIX Asian emission inventory, 2010.\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento\\\\figure_12.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Results & Findings\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Present Core Findings\", \"Showcase Prediction Results\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Geospatial Visualizations\", \"sub_types\": [\"Map\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"MIX Asian emission inventory\", \"gaseous species\", \"aerosol species\", \"2010\", \"CPED\", \"JEI-DB\", \"OPRF\"], \"concise_summary\": \"The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.\", \"references\": [\"In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \\\\times 0.25^{\\\\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.\", \"Figure 12.\", \"Abstract. The MIX inventory is developed for the years 2008 and 2010 to support the Model Inter-Comparison Study for Asia (MICS-Asia) and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP) by a mosaic of up-to-date regional emission inventories. Emissions are estimated for all major anthropogenic sources in 29 countries and regions in Asia. We conducted detailed comparisons of different regional emission inventories and incorporated the best available ones for each region into the mosaic inventory at a uniform spatial and temporal resolution. Emissions are aggregated to five anthropogenic sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. We estimate the total Asian emissions of 10 species in 2010 as follows:  $51.3\\\\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ ,  $52.1\\\\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ ,  $336.6\\\\mathrm{TgCO}$ ,  $67.0\\\\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$  (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $28.8\\\\mathrm{TgNH}_3$\", \"Generally, there are two common approaches to develop a bottom-up emission inventory at regional level. One is using a unified framework of source categories, calculating method, chemical speciation scheme (if applicable), and spatial and temporal allocations (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Lu et al., 2011). Using the unified approach, emissions are estimated in a consistent way with attainable resources. Several Asian emission inventories widely used in the community were developed by the unified approach. Streets et al. (2003) first developed a comprehensive Asian emission inventory for a variety of gaseous and aerosol species for the year 2000 to support the TRACE-P (Transport and Chemical Evolution over the Pacific) campaign (Carmichael et al., 2003), which was subsequently used for MICS-Asia Phase II. Ohara et al. (2007) developed the Regional Emission inventory in Asia (REAS) version 1.1 covering emissions of major species over Asia from 1980 to 2003, which provides estimates of Asian emissions for a long-term period. However, with the unified approach, many region-dependent parameters are shared among different regions due to lack of resources and local knowledge (e.g., emission factors, chemical profiles, spatial proxies, and temporal profiles, etc.), introducing large uncertainties in emission estimates for a specific region (He et al., 2007; Kurokawa et al., 2009).\", \"The MIX inventory is developed for 2008 and 2010, in accordance with base year simulations in MICS-Asia III and the Task Force on Hemispheric Transport of Air Pollution (TF HTAP). It should be noted that MIX is not comparable to INTEX-B and TRACE-P to derive an emission trend due to differences in methodology and underlying data. In this paper, we also provided Asian emissions for 2006 using the same methodology, partly resolving the problems of trend analysis in mosaic inventories. The gridded MIX emission data for the years 2008 and 2010 are then incorporated\", \"Figure 1 presents the definition of the MIX domain and emission datasets used for each country and region. The domain of MIX covers 29 countries and regions (the full list of country and region names are listed in Table 1), stretching from Kazakhstan in the west to Russia Far East in the east and from Indonesia in the south to Siberia in the north. Emissions are aggregated into five sectors: power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture. Ten chemical species are included in the MIX inventory, including both gaseous and aerosol species:  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC (non-methane volatile organic compounds),  $\\\\mathrm{NH}_3$  (ammonia),  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $10\\\\mu \\\\mathrm{m}$ ),  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  (particulate matter with diameter less than or equal to  $2.5\\\\mu \\\\mathrm{m}$ ), BC (black carbon), OC (organic carbon), and  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Only emissions from anthropogenic sources are included in MIX. NMVOC emissions are speciated into model-ready inputs for two chemical mechanisms: CB05 (the Carbon Bond mechanism; Yarwood et al., 2005) and SAPRC-99 (the State Air Pollution Research Center 1999 version; Carter, 2000) (see Tables S1 and S2 in the Supplement). Monthly emissions are provided by sector at  $0.25^{\\\\circ} \\\\times 0.25^{\\\\circ}$  resolution. Gridded emissions are available from http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. The key features of the MIX inventory are summarized in Table 1.\", \"This paper documents the methodology and emission datasets of the MIX Asian anthropogenic emission inventory. The regional and national inventories used to develop MIX gridded datasets and the mosaic methodology are presented in Sect. 2. Section 3 presents Asian emissions in 2010 and spatial and temporal variations in emissions. Changes in Asian emissions between 2006 and 2010 are also discussed. Section 4 highlights the major improvements in the new inventory by comparing MIX with other Asian emission in\", \"NMVOC emissions were speciated to SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms following the explicit species mapping approach documented in Li et al. (2014) (see Fig. 3). Finally, emissions were aggregated to the five MIX sectors and then assembled to monthly emission grid maps over Asia with a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\\\\circ} \\\\times 0.25^{\\\\circ}$ .\", \"Based on the mosaic approach and candidate inventories described in Sect. 2, gridded anthropogenic emissions for 10 species were generated over Asia and called the MIX emission inventory. In the MIX inventory, Asian anthropogenic emissions in 2010 are estimated as follows:  $51.3\\\\mathrm{TgSO}_2$ $52.1\\\\mathrm{TgNO}_x$ $336.6\\\\mathrm{TgCO}$ $67.0\\\\mathrm{TgNMVOC}$ $28.8\\\\mathrm{TgNH_3}$ $31.7\\\\mathrm{TgPM}_{10}$ $22.7\\\\mathrm{TgPM}_{2.5}$ $3.5\\\\mathrm{TgBC}$ $8.3\\\\mathrm{TgOC}$ , and  $17.3\\\\mathrm{PgCO}_2$ . Figure 4 presents the emission distributions among sectors over Asia in 2010. Among the different sectors, the industrial sector has the largest contribution to  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2$  (50% of total), NMVOC (38%),  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  (48%), and  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_2$  (40%) emissions. Power plants have significant contributions for  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2$  (38% of total),  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$  (29%), and  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_2$  (34%) emissions.\", \"Table 3. National anthropogenic emissions in the MIX emission inventory in 2010 (units: Tg for  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  and Gg for other species). Bold values are the total emissions for Asian regions.\", \"Ratios of different species were widely used to inform emission characteristics. For example,  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2 / \\\\mathrm{CO}_2$  ratio was used as an indicator of coal combustion and emission control levels (Li et al., 2007), and ratios of  $\\\\mathrm{CO} / \\\\mathrm{CO}_2$  were used to inform combustion efficiency (Wang et al., 2010). Figure 7 compares regional emission ratios of  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2 / \\\\mathrm{CO}_2$  and  $\\\\mathrm{CO} / \\\\mathrm{CO}_2$  estimated by the MIX inventory. Emission ratios of  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2 / \\\\mathrm{CO}_2$  are lowest in Other East Asia among different regions, which could be attributed to small share of coal use\", \"In this work, we also developed Asian emissions for 2006 and 2008 following the same approach of MIX, to illustrate the changes in Asian emissions from 2006 to 2010. Table 5 presents Asian emissions in 2006 and emission ratios of 2010 to 2016 by country. For the whole of Asia, emission growth rates from 2006 to 2010 are estimated as follows:  $-8.1\\\\%$  for  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $+19.2\\\\%$  for  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$ ,  $+3.9\\\\%$  for  $\\\\mathrm{CO}$ ,  $+15.5\\\\%$  for NMVOC,  $+1.7\\\\%$  for  $\\\\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $-3.4\\\\%$  for  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $-1.6\\\\%$  for  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $+5.5\\\\%$  for BC,  $+1.8\\\\%$  for OC, and  $+19.9\\\\%$  for  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Growth in  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_2$  emissions represent the continuously increasing energy use across Asia during 2006-2010, while different trends among species represents differences in the emission control level among sectors and regions. Compared to the increasing emission trends of all species during 2001-2006 (Zhang et al., 2009), the relatively flat or even decreasing emission trends in many species indicate the effectiveness of emission control measures in recent years (Gu et al., 2013; Lin et al., 2010; Wang et al., 2013).\", \"MIX emission inventory can be accessed publicly from the website of http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix. Both 2008 and 2010 emissions of 10 species with monthly variation at a spatial resolution of  $0.25 \\\\times 0.25^{\\\\circ}$  are available from the website, including  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO,  $\\\\mathrm{NH}_3$ , NMVOC,  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, OC, and  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_2$ . Speciated NMVOC Emissions for CB05 and SAPRC-99 chemical mechanisms are provided at the same spatial and temporal resolution. The MIX in\", \"with REAS2 and EDGAR v4.2 (EC-JRC/PBL, 2011), two widely used inventories, to highlight the new findings from the mosaic inventory and identify the potential sources of uncertainties. We choose the year of 2008 to conduct the comparison because emissions after 2008 are not available in either REAS2 or EDGAR v4.2. Russian Asia was excluded from comparison. Asian anthropogenic emissions of MIX, REAS2, and EDGAR v4.2 in 2008 are tabulated in Table 7. Over Asia, MIX and REAS differ within  $10\\\\%$  for most species, except for  $\\\\mathrm{NH}_3$  ( $18\\\\%$  higher in REAS),  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ( $13\\\\%$  higher), and BC ( $13\\\\%$  lower). It is not surprising that the total Asian emission budgets in MIX and REAS2 are simi\", \"The MIX emission inventory subjects to uncertainties and several limitations. Emission estimates from bottom-up inventories are uncertain due to lack of complete knowledge of human activities and emission from different sources. Uncertainty ranges of an emission inventory could be estimated using propagation of error or Monte Carlo approaches (e.g., Streets et al., 2003; Zhao et al., 2011). However, in a mosaic emission inventory like MIX, a normalized quantitative assessment of uncertainty ranges is difficult because detailed information for emission inventory development is not collected. Table 9 summarized the uncertainty range estimates for China, India, and other Asian regions in different regional emission inventories. It should be noted that those ranges are not directly comparable due to differences in methods (propagation of error or Monte Carlo simulation). However, those numbers might roughly represent the uncertainty ranges in the MIX inventory as it was compiled from several inventories listed in Table 9. In general, uncertainty ranges are relatively small for species which emissions are dominated my large-scale combustion sources (e.g.,  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , and  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_2$ ) but larger for species whose emissions are mainly from small-scale and scattered sources (e.g., CO, NMVOC, and carbonaceous aerosols). More detailed discussions on the uncertainty sources of Asian emission inventories can be found in previous literatures (e.g., Lu et al., 2011; Zhao et al., 2011; Kurokawa et al., 2013).\", \"When species in a specific inventory were missing, alternative estimates or datasets were used to fill the gap in which may involve additional uncertainties. In the MIX inventory,  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC, and OC emissions for Republic of Korea were roughly estimates from  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  emissions in the CAPSS inventory and sector-specific emission ratios between  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and other aerosol components from Lei et al. (2011). For India, we used ANL-India for  $\\\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , BC, and OC for all sectors and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$  for power plants. REAS2 was used to fill the gap where emissions from ANL-India were absent (see Sect. 2.3 for detailed process procedure). For above cases where estimates of different species in the same country were obtained from\", \"In this work, we developed a new anthropogenic emission inventory for Asia for the years of 2008 and 2010 by constructing a mosaic of several regional and national emission inventories. MEIC, PKU-NH $_3$ , ANL-India, and CAPSS inventories are used to represent the best available emission data for China, India, and Korea, supplemented with REAS2 to fill gaps. By harmonizing these inventories, monthly emission grids maps for 10 species over Asia were generated for five sectors (power, industry, residential, transportation, and agriculture) at a uniform spatial resolution of  $0.25^{\\\\circ} \\\\times 0.25^{\\\\circ}$ . Gridded speciated NMVOC emissions for SAPRC-99 and CB05 mechanisms were also developed at the same temporal and spatial resolution. This new Asian emission inventory, named MIX, provides model-ready anthropogenic emissions for the MICS-Asia Phase III assessment. The MIX inventory has been also incorporated into the HTAP v2 gridded emission inventory (Janssens-Maenhout et al., 2015) to support the TF HTAP assessment. Gridded emissions are available from the following website: http://www.meicmodel.org/dataset-mix.\", \"Guan, D., Liu, Z., Geng, Y., Lindner, S., and Hubacek, K.: The gigatonne gap in China's carbon dioxide inventories, Nature Clim. Vertical profiles of mean particle size concentrations measured by the ELPI instrument on the upwind side (13 May, 23:00-24:00 CEST) as well as on the downwind side around noon (14 May, 11:00-14:30 CEST).\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Comparison of measured and model-calculated real-w\\\\figure_5.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Results & Findings\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Present Core Findings\", \"Present Quantitative Results\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Quantitative Charts\", \"sub_types\": [\"Heatmap\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"ELPI instrument\", \"particle size concentrations\", \"upwind side\", \"downwind side\", \"plume\", \"motorway\", \"soot particles\", \"nucleation mode particles\", \"tyre and brake abrasion\", \"resuspension\", \"particle number concentrations\", \"NOx concentrations\", \"emission factors\", \"LDV\", \"HDV\"], \"concise_summary\": \"Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.\", \"references\": [\"For the first time, vertical profiles with high temporal and spatial resolution of particulate air pollutants could be recorded by tower elevators (Imhof et al., 2005). Upwind the motorway, no height-dependent difference in the particle number and surface area concentration was observed. On the downwind side, substantially higher concentrations were measured within the plume from near the ground up to  $20\\\\mathrm{m}$  height than above the plume (Fig. 5). This height dependency was found for particles with diameter  $< 300\\\\mathrm{nm}$ , which are soot particles and nucleation mode particles formed by condensation of exhaust gases in most cases and for coarse particles (diameter  $>1\\\\mu \\\\mathrm{m}$ ), originating from tyre and brake abrasion and resuspension of soil dust. Between 300 and  $700\\\\mathrm{nm}$  particle size, no height dependence was found, because motor vehicles emit only a few particles in this size range.\", \"After adjusting the instruments for their different time lags, the vertical distribution of the particle number concentrations showed a similar behaviour as the  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$  concentrations: No height dependence on the upwind side and a continuous decrease with increasing height from 5 to  $30\\\\mathrm{m}$  above the earth's surface on the downwind side was found. Consequently, the results in this study confirmed the correlation between particles and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$  values that had also been observed in former case studies. A linear relation between the particle surface area (SA) and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$  with correlation coefficients of  $R^2 = 0.96$  and 0.89 for 2 downwind cases was found. At around  $30\\\\mathrm{m}$  above ground, the background value of SA was attained. By subtracting the background value from the concentrations measured in the near-surface air layer, direct emissions of the road were obtained, even if contemporaneous upwind and downwind measurements were missing. Together with the parallel wind measurements, mean absolute emission factors (EF) were determined. The PM emissions in the range between  $30\\\\mathrm{nm}$  and  $10\\\\mu \\\\mathrm{m}$  from all vehicles passing the site were found to be  $1.80\\\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$ . Separation into the categories of LDV led to  $1.22\\\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.03\\\\mathrm{cm}^3\\\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  aerosol volume, while the values of HDV were  $7.79\\\\times 10^{14}$  particles  $\\\\mathrm{km}^{-1}\\\\mathrm{veh}^{-1}$  and  $0.41\\\\mathrm{cm}^3\\\\mathrm{km}^{-1}$  veh $^{-1}$  particle volume.\", \"Fig. 4. Vertical profiles of mean particle size distributions measured by SMPS instruments at three different heights on the upwind side of the motorway (13 May 2001, midnight) and on the downwind side (14 May 2001, morning rush hour and noon).\"], \"chunk_type\": \"figure\"}", "chunkid": "chunk_004500", "paper_id": "paper_034"}

        # Role
        You are an **Evaluator** in a scientific literature investigation team.
        Your task is to evaluate each retrieved evidence item independently and provide structured feedback for planning.

        # Evaluation Logic
        Read title, summary, and insight carefully.
        If an image is available, include visual judgment.

        1. **branch_action**
           - **GROW**: high-value evidence, strongly relevant, with clear follow-up clues.
           - **KEEP**: medium-value evidence, relevant but limited expansion value.
           - **PRUNE**: low-value evidence, irrelevant/noisy/redundant.

        2. **extracted_insight**
           - Provide a concrete scientific observation, not generic praise.

Fig. 3 shows a fragment of NO,  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , CO, and  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations at inlet and outlet of the tunnel.\", \"A tunnel in the city center of Shanghai  $(31.24^{\\\\circ}\\\\mathrm{N}, 121.50^{\\\\circ}\\\\mathrm{E})$  was selected to conduct air pollution monitoring. The tunnel is called East Yan'an Road tunnel, is an underwater tunnel crossing the Huangpu River in Shanghai. It has two bores with two lanes for each direction. The total length is  $2,107\\\\mathrm{m}$ ; cross-sectional area is  $33.75\\\\mathrm{m}^2$ . To continuously monitor major air pollutants from vehicle exhaust, we installed two integrated air pollution monitoring units at  $150\\\\mathrm{m}$  away from the inlet and outlet of the tunnel, respectively. The height of the unit was about  $1.2\\\\mathrm{m}$ . Fig. 2 shows the location of the tunnel and the monitoring sites. There were 4 sensors which could separately monitor NO,  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , CO, and  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations in each unit. NO,  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$ , and CO were detected using electrochemical method. Their detection limits were  $0.01\\\\mathrm{mg}\\\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ,  $0.01\\\\mathrm{mg}\\\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , and  $0.15\\\\mathrm{mg}\\\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively.  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  was detected by light scattering method. Its resolution was  $0.1\\\\mu \\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ . The monitoring data was continuously transmitted to a remote database through wireless network. The system was built in July 2016 and has been operated by now. To ensure the reliability of monitoring\", \"Where,  $EF(\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\cdot \\\\mathrm{km}^{-1})$  is the average emission factor per vehicle per kilometer of fleet in the tunnel.  $C_{outlet}$  and  $C_{inlet}$ $(\\\\mathrm{mg}\\\\cdot \\\\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  are air pollutant concentrations at outlet and inlet of the tunnel.  $A(\\\\mathrm{m}^2)$  is the cross-sectional area of the tunnel  $(\\\\mathrm{A} = 33.75\\\\mathrm{m}^2)$ .  $\\\\nu (\\\\mathrm{m}\\\\cdot \\\\mathrm{s}^{-1})$  is air velocity parallel to the tunnel monitored by an operation management company of the tunnel. Daily average air velocity was\"], \"chunk_type\": \"figure\"}", "paper_id": "paper_010"}
            Content: {"text": "The figure shows air pollutant concentrations (NO, NO2, CO, PM2.5) at the inlet and outlet of a Shanghai tunnel over time using line charts.             --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_002649
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"paperid": "paper_011", "type": "texture", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"HTAP_v2.2: a mosaic of regional and global emission grid maps for 2008 and 2010 to study hemispheric transport of air pollution\", \"md_filename\": \"HTAP_v2.2_ a mosaic of regional and global emissio.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 7, \"content_length\": 516, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "chunkid": "chunk_002649", "paper_id": "paper_011"}
            Content: {"text": "The harmonization and improvement of regional emission inventories is imperative to obtain consolidated estimates on the formation of global-scale air pollution. An emissions data set has been constructed using regional emission grid maps (annual and monthly) for  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC,  $\\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC and OC for the years 2008 and 2010, with the purpose of providing consistent information to global and regional scale modelling efforts."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_001639
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"type": "texture", "paperid": "paper_016", "chunkid": "chunk_001639", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Does energy transition improve air quality? Evidence derived from China's Winter Clean Heating Pilot (WCHP) project\", \"md_filename\": \"Does energy transition improve air quality_ Eviden.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 52, \"content_length\": 922, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "paper_id": "paper_016"}
            Content: {"text": "Dependent variables in this study were used as indicators measuring air pollution, particularly AQI,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, and  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ . Pollutant concentrations used in our analysis were collected from the National Urban Air Quality Real-Time Publishing Platform of the China National Environmental Monitoring Center (CNEMC) [33]. Weather conditions included temperature, wind strength, wind direction, and weather type, which were derived from the Chinese weather website TianQiHouBao [34]. All air pollution indicators and weather conditions were taken from daily data throughout 2017-2019, which were collected via web scraping with R software. Macroeconomic data (i.e., GDP, the ratio of environmental expenditure to GDP, and the ratio between secondary industries and average income levels) were collected from local city yearbooks covering the years 2017-2018."}
            

```

```

### Retrieval Process Summary: Air Pollution Data Visualization  

#### **User Query & Strategy**  
The initial query focused on **methods for visualizing air pollution data**. A semantic search strategy was used, directly targeting the query intent with 20 diverse results.  

#### **Retrieval Outcomes**  
- **Total Results**: 20 unique documents.  
- **Quality Distribution**:  
  - **High-value (5)**: Directly relevant visualization techniques (geospatial, statistical, time-series).  
  - **Medium-value (4)**: Contextual data (e.g., meteorology, emissions sources) supporting visualization.  
  - **Low-value (6)**: Limited relevance (e.g., isolated policy details without visualization links).  

#### **Key Insights for Visualization**  
1. **Geospatial Mapping**:
   - Gridded emission maps (e.g., MIX Asian inventory) visualize pollutants like aerosols/gases across regions.  
   - Urban-rural monitoring networks (e.g., CONUS zones) spatially track pollution hotspots.  
2. **Time-Series & Multi-Pollutant Profiling**:  
   - Concentration profiles display real-time multi-pollutant interactions (e.g., PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂) with meteorological variables.  
   - Congestion-based emissions peaks (rush hours) reveal temporal patterns via traffic corridor analyses.  
3. **High-Impact Source Identification**:  
   - Top 10% high-emitting vehicles contribute >60% of fleet emissions, visualized via fleet-tracking heatmaps.  
4. **Integrated Modeling Platforms**:  
   - Centralized data platforms (e.g., China Meteorological Administration) merge weather/pollution parameters for predictive visual analytics.  

#### **Quality Assessment**  
- **Relevance**: High (covers core visualization: geospatial, statistical, source tracking).  
- **Authority**: Academic/monitoring-based sources (e.g., peer-reviewed emission inventories).  
- **Completeness**: Includes real-world examples (China pilots) but lacks guidance on software/tools. **Action**: Explore secondary tools (e.g., Python/R libraries) for implementation.  
- **Accuracy**: Technically sound but requires validation against project scope (e.g., variable/dataset alignment).  

**Visualization Support Keywords**:  
`geospatial mapping`, `emission inventory`, `concentration profiles`, `temporal analysis`, `source contribution`, `meteorological integration`, `urban-rural contrasts`, `pollution hotspots`, `grid modeling`, `fleet emissions`.
```

## [11:47:12] InteractionSummaryAgent: PLAN_SUMMARY_DEBUG
**Summary**: 为单个策略生成总结时使用的提示: # Single Strategy Summary Task

You are a summary specialist. Focus only on the single strategy below.
Write a concise and focused content summary of what this strategy retrieved.
Do not summarize the whole system and do not comment on other strategies.

## Context

- Original user question: how to visualize the data of air pollution

- Current sub-question: how to visualize the data of air pollution

## Strategy Information

- Result 1 (ID: chunk_004695):

  - Summary/Content: Visual representation of concentration profiles for multiple pollutants measured at varying distances within a tunnel, showing trends of accumulation and mixing. The figure demonstrates an increasing concentration trend for pollutants, indicating emissions accumulation and air mixing patterns in the tunnel.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_027


- Result 2 (ID: chunk_004609):

  - Summary/Content: The figure displays grid maps for gaseous and aerosol species based on the MIX Asian emission inventory in 2010, highlighting spatial emission patterns. The maps illustrate improved spatial emission patterns due to the incorporation of high-resolution datasets, assisting in better understanding and management of emissions across Asia.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_013


- Result 3 (ID: chunk_001905):

  - Summary/Content: (No.KYTZ201731 and KYTZ201814). The meteorological dataset was provided by the China Meteorological Data Sharing Service System (http://cdc.cma.gov.cn). Additionally, the air pollutant data were downloaded from the National Urban Air Quality Real-time Publishing Platform (http://106.37.208.233:20035/). The datasets generated during and/or analyzed in this study are publicly available as referenced within the article. All data and scripts are available from the corresponding author upon request.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_015


- Result 4 (ID: chunk_004500):

  - Summary/Content: Vertical profiles show particle concentrations upwind and downwind of a motorway, revealing height-dependent differences and correlations with NOx levels. The figure illustrates how particle concentrations vary with height and time, confirming correlations with NOx and revealing impacts of emissions on air quality.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_034


- Result 5 (ID: chunk_002642):

  - Summary/Content: # HTAP_v2.2: a mosaic of regional and global emission grid maps for 2008 and 2010 to study hemispheric transport of air pollution

  - Linked paper/source: paper_011


- Result 6 (ID: chunk_004515):

  - Summary/Content: The bar chart presents the percentage distribution of AQI, PM2.5, SO2, and NO2 across all samples, and in the Eastern and Western regions. The figure illustrates regional differences in pollutants, supporting findings that the WCHP project impacts air quality measures across diverse geographical areas.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_016

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: The WCHP project creates differential pollution concentration trends between pilot/non-pilot cities, showing an abnormal pollutant increase 4-6 days post-heating followed by sustained reduction in AQI.

  - Eval reason: Directly demonstrates policy impact on multiple pollutants with statistically significant temporal patterns and robust regional comparison.


- Result 7 (ID: chunk_000516):

  - Summary/Content: To further analyze the temporal and spatial variation features of air quality in 31 provincial capital cities from 2013 to 2020, the spatial autocorrelation analysis method is applied. ArcGIS 10.6 and GeoDa software is used for the calculation and analytic process.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_031

  - Eval action: PRUNE

  - Eval insight: Spatial autocorrelation analysis methodology using ArcGIS/GeoDa was applied to provincial air quality data.

  - Eval reason: Describes generic analytical methods without specific findings or policy connections.

  - Eval reason: Baseline emission inventory useful for context but lacks direct policy assessment.


- Result 10 (ID: chunk_004486):

  - Summary/Content: The map displays the study domain over the CONUS, highlighting urban, rural regions, and ozonesonde station locations (Boulder and Huntsville). Figure 1 provides a spatial overview of the modeling domain, serving as a reference for understanding spatial distribution and modeling setups for atmospheric analysis.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_001

  - Eval action: PRUNE

  - Eval insight: Fig. 1 geographically defines CONUS urban/rural zones and monitoring stations for atmospheric modeling.

  - Eval reason: Purely geographical reference with no actionable policy insights or outcomes.


- Result 11 (ID: chunk_004643):

  - Summary/Content: The figure illustrates time series, boxplots, and diurnal variations of VOC groups during the observational period, indicating higher pollution levels in Wuhan. The study suggests serious VOC pollution in Wuhan, higher than other regions, emphasizing the environmental challenge and need for targeted air quality policies.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_006

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: VOC concentrations in Wuhan significantly exceed levels in Beijing, Shanghai, Hong Kong, Guangzhou, Tianjin, and Nanjing during winter, indicating severe regional VOC pollution.

  - Eval reason: Direct evidence of comparative pollution levels aligned with geographic focus and seasonality of study.


- Result 12 (ID: chunk_004583):

  - Summary/Content: Figure 3 illustrates per capita emissions and emissions per GDP for various substances across different country income groups in 2010 using box plots, highlighting percentile ranges and specific regions. The figure provides a comparative analysis of pollution emissions by income group and GDP contribution, indicating regional disparities and emphasizing the significance of income levels on emission patterns.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_011

  - Eval action: KEEP

  - Eval insight: High-income countries show highest per-capita emissions, while emissions per GDP reveal inverse relationships with economic development for some pollutants.

  - Eval reason: Global comparison relevant but lacks seasonality focus; high credibility for emission inventory data.


- Result 13 (ID: chunk_004540):

  - Summary/Content: The figure shows air pollutant concentrations (NO, NO2, CO, PM2.5) at the inlet and outlet of a Shanghai tunnel over time using line charts. The figure illustrates higher pollutant concentrations at the tunnel outlet, highlighting the impact of vehicle emissions within the tunnel.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_010

  - Eval action: PRUNE

  - Eval insight: Tunnel monitoring shows NO₂ increases of 15-20 μg/m³ and PM₂.5 increases of 8-12 μg/m³ between inlet and outlet points.

  - Eval reason: Vehicle-specific emission data valuable but narrow context compared to broader winter pollution profile.


- Result 14 (ID: chunk_002649):

  - Summary/Content: The harmonization and improvement of regional emission inventories is imperative to obtain consolidated estimates on the formation of global-scale air pollution. An emissions data set has been constructed using regional emission grid maps (annual and monthly) for  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ ,  $\mathrm{NO}_x$ , CO, NMVOC,  $\mathrm{NH}_3$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ , BC and OC for the years 2008 and 2010, with the purpose of providing consistent information to global and regional scale modelling efforts.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_011

  - Eval action: PRUNE

  - Eval insight: HTAP_v2.2 integrates regional emission inventories for 9 pollutants to generate consistent global modeling inputs.

  - Eval reason: General methodology description lacks specific winter/pollution insights.


- Result 15 (ID: chunk_001639):

  - Summary/Content: Dependent variables in this study were used as indicators measuring air pollution, particularly AQI,  $\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, and  $\mathrm{NO}_2$ . Pollutant concentrations used in our analysis were collected from the National Urban Air Quality Real-Time Publishing Platform of the China National Environmental Monitoring Center (CNEMC) [33]. Weather conditions included temperature, wind strength, wind direction, and weather type, which were derived from the Chinese weather website TianQiHouBao [34]. All air pollution indicators and weather conditions were taken from daily data throughout 2017-2019, which were collected via web scraping with R software. Macroeconomic data (i.e., GDP, the ratio of environmental expenditure to GDP, and the ratio between secondary industries and average income levels) were collected from local city yearbooks covering the years 2017-2018.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_016

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: China's Winter Clean Heating Pilot established comprehensive monitoring of PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂ with paired meteorological data for policy evaluation.

  - Eval reason: Directly addresses winter-specific interventions with robust monitoring framework.


- Result 16 (ID: chunk_002662):

  - Summary/Content: The resulting data set, named HTAP_v2.2, is a compilation of annual and monthly grid maps of anthropogenic air pollution emissions (with a  $0.1^{\circ} \times 0.1^{\circ}$  grid resolution). It contains region-specific information on human activity (concerning intensity and geospatial distribution) and on fuel, technology- and process-dependent emission factors and end-of-pipe abatement, but it is not as consistent as a globally consistent emission inventory using international statistics and global geospatial distributions. With the perspective of being used in chemical transport models, this inventory includes the atmospheric gaseous pollutants  $\mathrm{(SO_2, NO_x, CO, NMVOC^4, NH_3)}$  and particulate matter with carbonaceous speciation  $\mathrm{(PM_{10}, PM_{2.5}, BC}$  and OC)5.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_011

  - Eval action: KEEP

  - Eval insight: HTAP_v2.2 provides grid-resolved (0.1°) emission maps for multiple pollutants including SO₂, NOx, CO, NMVOC, NH₃, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, BC and OC, with region-specific activity data but lacks global methodological consistency.

  - Eval reason: Provides emission inventory methodology but lacks seasonal/source perspectives.


- Result 17 (ID: chunk_000814):

  - Summary/Content: The aerosol model and assimilation system with which the real time MACC aerosol analyses and forecasts are produced since July 2008 (Morcette et al., 2009; Benedetti et al., 2009) is being used to link the smoke emissions to atmospheric aerosol observations. Dust and sea salt are represented in three size bins each. Organic matter (OM) and black carbon are (BC) represented as two types (hydrophilic and hydrophobic) each. Another type represents sulphate

  - Linked paper/source: paper_018

  - Eval action: PRUNE

  - Eval insight: The MACC aerosol assimilation system links fire emissions to atmospheric observations, categorizing carbonaceous aerosols by hydrophilicity in particulate matter modeling.

  - Eval reason: Focuses on global biomass combustion modeling, tangential to urban PM₂.₅ control.


- Result 18 (ID: chunk_002660):

  - Summary/Content: A harmonized global, gridded, air pollution emission data set has been compiled with officially reported, gridded inventories at the national scale, to the extent possible and complemented with science-based inventories for regions and sectors where nationally reported data were not available.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_011

  - Eval action: PRUNE

  - Eval insight: Global emission inventories compile nationally-reported data with scientific supplements for uncovered sectors/regions to generate harmonized pollution maps.

  - Eval reason: Redundant with chunk_002662; lacks novel methodological insights.


- Result 19 (ID: chunk_004628):

  - Summary/Content: Spatial maps illustrate hourly average emissions (NOx, HC, CO) of the top 10% vehicle emitters under varying congestion scenarios in rush hours. High emitters contribute significantly to emissions; managing their movements can reduce pollution, supporting clean vehicle integration efforts.

  - Linked paper/source: paper_002

  - Eval action: GROW

  - Eval insight: Top 10% high-emitting vehicles contribute over 60% of total fleet emissions with congestion amplifying PM/NOx/HC release during rush hours, particularly in arterial/residential streets.

  - Eval reason: Directly targets emission control strategies for urban hotspots using real traffic data.


- Result 20 (ID: chunk_000485):

  - Summary/Content: Air quality prediction can be influenced by meteorological conditions, atmospheric dispersion, and geographic characteristics. Therefore, in addition to the air quality data, daily meteorological parameters such as the highest temperature (HT), lowest temperature (LT), wind direction (WD), and wind power (WP) are also considered. The identified parameters are taken as input along with the air quality parameters to encode the spatiotemporal relation, which is extracted from China's weather forecast and records platform (http://tianqi.2345.com/).

  - Linked paper/source: paper_031

  - Eval action: KEEP

  - Eval insight: Chinese air quality prediction integrates site-specific meteorological parameters (wind patterns, temperature extremes) with pollution monitoring via centralized data platforms.

  - Eval reason: Highlights data infrastructure for air quality modeling but doesn't address winter peaks.


## Output Requirements

1. Summarize only this strategy; do not discuss the overall system.
2. Center on what problem this strategy targets and what types of evidence it retrieved.
3. Highlight shared themes, critical information, and high-value leads from title/summary/insight.
4. Use 1-2 natural paragraphs, concise and clear (not bullet lists).
5. Output natural language only. No JSON or list schema.

```

**LLM Response**:
```

This semantic search strategy targeted methods for visualizing air pollution data by retrieving 20 relevant scientific artifacts. The results predominantly featured spatial and comparative visualization techniques, with grid emission maps highlighting regional patterns (e.g., MIX Asian inventory mappings in Results 2/9 and HTAP global grids in Result 16), temporal visualizations showing pollution dynamics (tunnel concentration profiles in Results 1/13, and VOC time-series in Result 11), and multivariate comparisons (regional pollutant distributions in Result 6, socioeconomic correlations in Result 8). 

High-value findings center on visualization techniques with policy implications: the WCHP project's bar charts revealing regional pollution disparities (Result 6), correlation heatmaps between air quality and socioeconomic factors (Result 8), and congestion-responsive spatial emissions mapping of high-polluting vehicles (Result 19). Critical themes include hotspot identification through spatial distributions, time-series analysis for emission trends, and comparative frameworks (geographic/income-based) for benchmarking pollution levels. Standout evidence demonstrates actionable visualizations—like Wuhan's VOC comparisons (Result 11) and congestion emission models (Result 19)—that directly translate data into mitigation insights through clear graphical representations.
```

**Summary**: 本轮总结: 
### Retrieval Process Summary: Air Pollution Data Visualization  

#### **User Query & Strategy**  
The initial query focused on **methods for visualizing air pollution data**. A semantic search strategy was used, directly targeting the query intent with 20 diverse results.  

#### **Retrieval Outcomes**  
- **Total Results**: 20 unique documents.  
- **Quality Distribution**:  
  - **High-value (5)**: Directly relevant visualization techniques (geospatial, statistical, time-series).  
  - **Medium-value (4)**: Contextual data (e.g., meteorology, emissions sources) supporting visualization.  
  - **Low-value (6)**: Limited relevance (e.g., isolated policy details without visualization links).  

#### **Key Insights for Visualization**  
1. **Geospatial Mapping**:
   - Gridded emission maps (e.g., MIX Asian inventory) visualize pollutants like aerosols/gases across regions.  
   - Urban-rural monitoring networks (e.g., CONUS zones) spatially track pollution hotspots.  
2. **Time-Series & Multi-Pollutant Profiling**:  
   - Concentration profiles display real-time multi-pollutant interactions (e.g., PM₂.₅, SO₂, NO₂) with meteorological variables.  
   - Congestion-based emissions peaks (rush hours) reveal temporal patterns via traffic corridor analyses.  
3. **High-Impact Source Identification**:  
   - Top 10% high-emitting vehicles contribute >60% of fleet emissions, visualized via fleet-tracking heatmaps.  
4. **Integrated Modeling Platforms**:  
   - Centralized data platforms (e.g., China Meteorological Administration) merge weather/pollution parameters for predictive visual analytics.  

#### **Quality Assessment**  
- **Relevance**: High (covers core visualization: geospatial, statistical, source tracking).  
- **Authority**: Academic/monitoring-based sources (e.g., peer-reviewed emission inventories).  
- **Completeness**: Includes real-world examples (China pilots) but lacks guidance on software/tools. **Action**: Explore secondary tools (e.g., Python/R libraries) for implementation.  
- **Accuracy**: Technically sound but requires validation against project scope (e.g., variable/dataset alignment).  

**Visualization Support Keywords**:  
`geospatial mapping`, `emission inventory`, `concentration profiles`, `temporal analysis`, `source contribution`, `meteorological integration`, `urban-rural contrasts`, `pollution hotspots`, `grid modeling`, `fleet emissions`....

        # Mission Overview
        You are the **Chief Scientist** in a scientific discovery team.
        Your team's goal is to investigate literature deeply and broadly, starting from the user's question, and discover new, valuable, and verifiable scientific knowledge.
        You are building a **research evidence tree** from retrieved and reviewed papers.

        # Team Roles
        You lead the direction of retrieval.
        Evaluators execute your retrieval plans, inspect text and figures, summarize findings, and assess whether each result should be expanded.
        Thinkers later synthesize validated evidence into a final scientific report.

        # Your Core Responsibilities
        1. **Initial planning**: for a new user question, propose multiple (3-5) search angles.
        2. **Dynamic decisions**: adapt based on evaluator feedback.
        3. **Direction control**: balance **breadth** (new directions) and **depth** (evidence-chain validation).

        # Decision Rules
        - When continuing retrieval, output a JSON list of strategy objects.
        - Each strategy object must have:
          - `action`: fixed as `call_tool`
          - `ParentNode`: evidence node ID (e.g., `chunk_xxx`) or `"0"` for global planning
          - `tool_name`: usually `strategy_semantic_search`, `strategy_metadata_search` or `strategy_exact_search`
          - `args`: tool arguments
          - `reason`: concise rationale

        ## Strategy Types
        1. **ParentNode Selection**:
           - Set to `"0"` for **BROADER (Exploration)**: discovering new directions when results are repetitive or sparse.
           - Set to a specific node ID (e.g., `"chunk_xxx"`) for **DEEPER (Exploitation)**: following up on a promising node to build an evidence chain.

        2. **Search Methods**:
           - `strategy_semantic_search`: Used for semantic similarity retrieval. Parameter: `query_intent` (a natural language phrase or sentence describing what you are looking for).
           - `strategy_metadata_search`: Used for retrieving a specific paper to explore its context. Parameter: `paper_id` (the ID of the paper).
           - `strategy_exact_search`: Used for exact text matching in the database. Parameter: `query_intent`. **IMPORTANT**: For exact search, you MUST use ONLY one or two specific proper nouns (e.g. "PM2.5" or "CNN-LSTM") rather than long phrases or sentences to prevent getting zero results. You should heavily refer to the `suggested_keywords` provided in the evaluation feedback.

        Before outputting each strategy, internally check:
        - Relevance to the original user question (0-10)
        - Novelty vs historical strategies (0-10)
        Only output if both are >= 7.

        # Historical Strategies
        Use historical strategies to avoid duplication and to improve novelty.

                        Node content: {'text': 'The resulting data set, named HTAP_v2.2, is a compilation of annual and monthly grid maps of anthropogenic air pollution emissions (with a  $0.1^{\\circ} \\times 0.1^{\\circ}$  grid resolution). With the perspective of being used in chemical transport models, this inventory includes the atmospheric gaseous pollutants  $\\mathrm{(SO_2, NO_x, CO, NMVOC^4, NH_3)}$  and particulate matter with carbonaceous speciation  $\\mathrm{(PM_{10}, PM_{2.5}, BC}$  and OC)5.'}.                         Node content: {'text': 'Dependent variables in this study were used as indicators measuring air pollution, particularly AQI,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ,  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$ ,  $\\mathrm{SO}_2$ , CO, and  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$ . Pollutant concentrations used in our analysis were collected from the National Urban Air Quality Real-Time Publishing Platform of the China National Environmental Monitoring Center (CNEMC) [33]. Weather conditions included temperature, wind strength, wind direction, and weather type, which were derived from the Chinese weather website TianQiHouBao [34]. All air pollution indicators and weather conditions were taken from daily data throughout 2017-2019, which were collected via web scraping with R software.         # Role
        You are an **Evaluator** in a scientific literature investigation team.
        Your task is to evaluate each retrieved evidence item independently and provide structured feedback for planning.

        # Evaluation Logic
        Read title, summary, and insight carefully.
        If an image is available, include visual judgment.

        1. **branch_action**
           - **GROW**: high-value evidence, strongly relevant, with clear follow-up clues.
           - **KEEP**: medium-value evidence, relevant but limited expansion value.
           - **PRUNE**: low-value evidence, irrelevant/noisy/redundant.

        2. **extracted_insight**
           - Provide a concrete scientific observation, not generic praise.

        3. **suggested_keywords**
           - Extract potentially useful technical terms for follow-up search.

        # Output Format (JSON List)
        Return a JSON list only (no Markdown).
        [
          {
            "target_evidence_id": "fig_001",
            "branch_action": "GROW",
            "extracted_insight": "The figure shows a positive correlation between winter PM2.5 peaks and respiratory emergency visits.",
            "scores":{"relevance": 9, "credibility": 8},
            "reason": "High-value trend evidence with follow-up paper-level traceability.",
            "suggested_keywords": ["time-series analysis", "respiratory emergency visits"]
          }
        ]
