            Metadata: {"type": "picture", "paperid": "paper_023", "chunkid": "chunk_004535", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Estimation of emissions from biomass burning in Ch\", \"figure_id\": \"img_91\", \"figure_title\": \"Figure 2. Spatial distribution of  CO_{2}  emissions (tonne) from each land-cover type (excluding small islands in the South China Sea).\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Estimation of emissions from biomass burning in Ch\\\\figure_2.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Results & Findings\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Present Core Findings\", \"Present Quantitative Results\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Geospatial Visualizations\", \"sub_types\": [\"Map\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"CO2 emissions\", \"biomass fire\", \"forest fire\", \"grassland fire\", \"cropland fire\", \"shrubland fire\", \"Yunnan-Guizhou Plateau\", \"Inner Mongolian Plateau\", \"Daxing'anling\", \"Xiaoxing'anling\", \"Tian Shan\", \"GlobeLand30\"], \"concise_summary\": \"The figure illustrates the spatial distribution of CO2 emissions from different land-cover types in China, excluding small islands in the South China Sea.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"The figure highlights areas with significant biomass fire emissions, correlating with land-cover types and geographic regions, to understand spatial emission patterns for regional air quality analysis.\", \"references\": [\"Nationwide spatial patterns of  $\\\\mathrm{CO}_{2}$  emissions from four sources are shown in Fig. 2 (biomass fire emissions from the small islands in the South China Sea are not included). Forest and grassland fire emissions were mainly distributed in northeastern China and southern China. Dense vegetative covers in the Yunnan-Guizhou Plateau, Inner Mongolian Plateau, Daxing'anling, Xiaoxing'anling, and the southeast hills greatly contribute to fire events. Cropland fire emissions were concentrated in the three great plains of China, namely the Northeast China Plain, the North China Plain, and the Middle-Lower Yangtze Plain. Because of high crop production in these areas, large quantities of agricultural residues were burned in fields during the short period following the harvest season. In addition, due to snowmelt in the Tian Shan, there are many oases located at the foot of the mountain range in Xinjiang Province. These oases are suitable for growing crops such as wheat and maize (Zhou et al., 2017). Therefore, crop fires emissions in Xinjiang province were higher than those in other northwestern provinces. Compared with other fire types, emissions from shrubland fire were negligible and the high emissions were concentrated in Guangdong and Yunnan provinces.\", \"In this study, we used fire radiative power (FRP) data derived from the MODIS active fire products to calculate emissions of 11 pollutants from biomass burning in China (excluding fires occurring on the small islands in the South China Sea) for the period of 2003-2017. The spatiotemporal distribution of emissions from four biomass burning types (forest, grassland, cropland, and shrubland fires) were studied in detail. A daily gridded  $1\\\\mathrm{km}$  emission inventory of biomass burning was established; this inventory could meet the requirements of global and regional air quality simulations.\", \"Monthly mean T/A ratios were calculated for each type of biomass burning. Different combustion characteristics of fuel types could be reflected by specific T/A ratios. As shown in Fig. S1 in the Supplement (excluding small islands in the South China Sea), China is divided into six temperature zones (tropical zone, subtropical zone, warm-temperate zone, middle-temperate zone, cold-temperate zone, and the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau). Because the dominant crop types vary greatly among temperature zones, we calculated T/A ratios for each zone separately. Using respective T/A ratios to calculate factors required in Eq. (1), the FRP diurnal cycle was parameterized for each zone and harvest season, which could reflect specific combustion characteristics of different straw types.\", \"The GlobeLand30 dataset maps global land cover at  $30\\\\mathrm{m}$  spatial resolution in two base years (2000 and 2010) (Chen et al., 2017b), as shown in Fig. S2 (small islands in the South China Sea are not included). GlobeLand30 data are generated by multispectral images derived from Landsat TM,  $\\\\mathrm{ETM + }$ , and the Chinese environmental disaster alleviation satellite Huanjing-1A (HJ-1). The result of the accuracy assessment shows that the overall accuracy of GlobeLand30 reaches  $83.5\\\\%$ . GlobeLand30 dataset consists of 10 land-cover types, namely cultivated land, forest, grassland, shrubland, wetland, water bodies, tundra, artificial surfaces, bare land, and permanent snow and ice. In this study, the land-cover types are characterized by GlobeLand30-2000 for years 2003-2005 and GlobeLand30-2010 for years 2006-2017. We combined the land-cover map of China and the latitude and longitude data of fire count in MOD14/MYD14 to determine the biomass fuel types. For instance, if a fire count was located in a cropland area, it was considered a crop residue burning event.\"], \"chunk_type\": \"figure\"}", "paper_id": "paper_023"}
            Content: {"text": "The figure illustrates the spatial distribution of CO2 emissions from different land-cover types in China, excluding small islands in the South China Sea. The figure highlights areas with significant biomass fire emissions, correlating with land-cover types and geographic regions, to understand spatial emission patterns for regional air quality analysis."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_002790
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"type": "texture", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"HTAP_v2.2: a mosaic of regional and global emission grid maps for 2008 and 2010 to study hemispheric transport of air pollution\", \"md_filename\": \"HTAP_v2.2_ a mosaic of regional and global emissio.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 166, \"content_length\": 1532, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "chunkid": "chunk_002790", "paperid": "paper_011", "paper_id": "paper_011"}
            Content: {"text": "This paper describes the HTAP global air pollutant reference emission inventory for 2010, which is composed of latest available data from regional inventory compilers. It assures a consistent input for both regional and global modelling as required by the HTAP modelling exercise. The HTAP_v2.2 emission database makes use of consolidated estimates of official and latest available regional information with air pollutant grid maps from US EPA and Environment Canada for North America, EMEP-TNO for Europe, MIX for Asia, and the EDGARv4.3 database for the rest of the world. The mosaic of grid maps provides comprehensive local information on the emission of air pollutants, because it results from the collection of point sources and national emission grid maps at  $0.1^{\\circ}$  (for some regions  $0.25^{\\circ}$ ) resolution. Even though the HTAP_v2.2 data set is not a self-consistent bottom-up database with activity data of consistent international statistics, with harmonized emission factors, and with global sets of spatial proxy data, it provides a unique set of emission grid maps with global coverage and high spatial resolution, including in particular important point sources. The compilation of implied emission factors and per capita emissions for the different world regions using multiple sources provides the regional and national emission inventory compilers with a valuable asset for comparison with their own data for cross checking and analysis which may lead to identification of future improvement options."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_000540
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Air quality changes in China 2013-2020: Effectiveness of clean coal technology policies\", \"md_filename\": \"Air quality changes in China 2013-2020_ Effectiven.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 104, \"content_length\": 501, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "type": "texture", "chunkid": "chunk_000540", "paperid": "paper_031", "paper_id": "paper_031"}
            Content: {"text": "After the spatial gatherings of air pollution are confirmed, to better identify the gathering district, a regional spatial autocorrelation analysis is also conducted. As shown in Fig. 5, there were still high-high gathering areas (high levels of pollution) and low-low gathering areas (low levels of pollution) from 2013 to 2020. Meanwhile, a north-south spatial heterogeneity pattern was obvious, depicting the high levels of pollution in northern China and low levels of pollution in southern China."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_004494
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"paperid": "paper_001", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\", \"figure_id\": \"img_50\", \"figure_title\": \"Fig. 9. Changes in the ozone production regime over the 5 boxes denoted in Fig. 8.\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the cont\\\\figure_9.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Results & Findings\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Present Core Findings\", \"Showcase Prediction Results\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Geospatial Visualizations\", \"sub_types\": [\"Map\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"ozone production regime\", \"metropolitan areas\", \"Seattle\", \"Portland\", \"Los Angeles\", \"San Francisco\", \"Dallas\", \"Houston\", \"Chicago\", \"Detroit\", \"Pittsburgh\", \"New York\"], \"concise_summary\": \"Figure 9 displays the ozone production regime changes in five metropolitan areas in the U.S. for the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, using geospatial maps.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"The maps illustrate shifts in ozone chemical regimes due to variations in NO2 and HCHO columns, highlighting the transition from NOx-saturated to transition or NOx-limited regimes in major cities.\", \"references\": [\"To examine the changes in the ozone chemical regime over major metropolitan cities more closely, we plotted the spatial distribution of the ozone production regime zoomed in over 5 boxes (Box A to Box E, denoted in Fig. 8) in Fig. 9, showing details of the selected metropolitan areas surrounding Seattle/Portland, Los Angeles/San Francisco, Dallas/Houston, Chicago, and Detroit/Pittsburgh/New York for the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017. In addition, Fig. 10 shows changes in  $\\\\mathrm{HCHO} / \\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio at urban cores for the major 12 cities over the CONUS. The change of the ozone production regime varied during the study period. As a response to the reduction of  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns in urban regions, which was greater than that of HCHO columns (Fig. S8), the clear shifts of the ozone chemical regime from  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated to transition, or transition to  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -\", \"# Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the contiguous United States inferred by the inversion of  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  and VOC emissions using satellite observation\", \"To investigate changes in the ozone  $(\\\\mathrm{O}_3)$  chemical production regime over the contiguous United States (CONUS) with accurate knowledge of concentrations of its precursors, we applied an inverse modeling technique with Ozone Monitoring Instrument (OMI) tropospheric nitrogen dioxide  $(\\\\mathrm{NO}_2)$  and total formaldehyde (HCHO) retrieval products in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which United States National Emission Inventory were based. The inclusion of dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions significantly account for the contribution of background sources in the free troposphere. Satellite-constrained nitrogen oxide  $(\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}})$  and non-methane volatile organic compounds (NMVOCs) emissions mitigate the discrepancy between satellite and modeled columns: the inversion suggested 2.33-2.84 (1.07-1.34) times higher  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  over the CONUS (over urban regions) and 0.28-0.81 times fewer NMVOCs emissions over the southeastern United States. The model-derived HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio shows gradual spatial changes in the  $\\\\mathrm{O}_3$  production regime near urban cores relative to previously defined threshold values representing  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  and VOC sensitive conditions. We also found apparent shifts from the  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime to the transition regime (or the transition regime to the  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime) over the major cities in the western United States. In contrast, rural areas, especially in the east-southeastern United States, exhibit a decreased HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio by  $-1.30 \\\\pm 1.71$  with a reduction in HCHO column primarily driven by meteorology, becoming sensitive to VOC emissions. Results show that incorporating satellite observations into numerical modeling could help policymakers implement appropriate emission control policies for  $\\\\mathrm{O}_3$  pollution.\", \"In this study, we applied an analytical inverse modeling technique with OMI  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO column retrievals and estimated top-down  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions over the contiguous U.S. (CONUS) in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, which were the base years that the U.S. NEI was formulated. From adjusted emissions, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS throughout the study period.\", \"Before investigating changes in the ozone chemical production regime, we adjusted total  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions with the OMI  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and HCHO column retrievals found by the inverse modeling approaches that we addressed in Section 2.4 in order to acquire accurate knowledge of the concentrations of ozone precursors. It is worth noting that we assumed the uncertainty of bottom-up emissions as the main contributor to the discrepancy between modeled (with prior emissions, dynamic boundary conditions, and LNO emissions) and satellite-observed columns; the discrepancy, however, could also be explained by several other deficiencies in chemical processes (e.g., a lifetime of alkyl nitrate) and meteorology (e.g., wind fields).\", \"# 3.3. Changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS\", \"threshold values by using the CMAQ model with posterior emission inventory based on the year 2011. Fig. 7 (a) represents the difference of averaged surface  $\\\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations between the baseline CMAQ model and the CMAQ with perturbed emission (20% reductions in  $\\\\mathrm{NO_x}$  and NMVOCs emissions) along with the corresponding modeled HCHO/NO $_2$  column ratio for the revisit time of the OMI satellite (13:00-14:00 LST) since we adjusted emissions of ozone precursors based on OMI column retrievals. In addition, the revisit time of the OMI satellite is beneficial to analyze the ozone chemical regime when the ozone production is maximum in a day in conjunction with the smallest solar zenith angle and most profound boundary layer height (Jin et al., 2017). To clearly examine the difference of  $\\\\mathrm{O_3}$  with perturbed emission, we considered model grids over polluted regions where the modeled tropospheric  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column is greater than  $1\\\\times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\\\\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$  (Boersma et al., 2016; Choi et al., 2012; Schaub et al., 2006). As a response to  $\\\\mathrm{NO_x}$  emission reduction, surface  $\\\\mathrm{O_3}$  concentrations generally increased proportionally to the HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio. The increase was dominant for a high HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio. The change in surface ozone concentrations became moderate for the case of NMVOCs emission reduction (red color); still, noticeable changes in ozone concentration were found for a low HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio.\", \"Fig. 8 (a) shows the spatial distribution of the ozone chemical production regime (based on the averaged HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio) over the CONUS in the summer of 2011, while the results for 2014 and 2017 are plotted in Fig. S11. The entire CONUS is divided into three regimes according to the threshold values we defined earlier; model grids with NO $_2$  column density less than  $1 \\\\times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\\\\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$  are not considered and are denoted by the gray color. Several metropolitan cities were categorized as  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated and transition regimes, whereas the remaining rural areas were classified as  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -limited regimes due to the nature of urban conditions with highly concentrated traffic density and amount of vehicle emissions. According to Fig. 8(b), showing the difference of HCHO/NO $_2$  ratio between 2011 and 2017, the column ratio in rural areas decreased by  $-1.30 \\\\pm 1.71$ , mostly over the eastern and southeastern U.S. This regime modification toward the  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$ -saturated condition could be explained by the increased  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions over the northeastern U.S. (Fig. S7), as well as the sustained decrease of the HCHO column (Fig. 3 (h-2)) as a response to meteorological change (Fig. S2).\", \"(b) Difference of HCHO/NO  ${}_{2}$  ratio between 2011 and 2017  \\nFig. 8. (a) Spatial distribution of the ozone chemical production regime over the CONUS for the year 2011. (b) The difference between the  $\\\\mathrm{HCHO / NO_2}$  ratios of 2011 and 2017. Gray areas represent tropospheric  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column densities that are less than  $1\\\\times 10^{15}$  molecules  $\\\\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$ .\", \"limited were found partially over metropolitan areas (e.g., Seattle, Houston, and Pittsburgh) or completely even at urban cores (e.g., Houston, San Francisco, and Salt Lake City), especially from 2014 to 2017. By contrast, Los Angeles, New York, and Portland were the cities steadily remaining in the  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime throughout the study period. Also, we observed the sudden expansions of  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated conditions at the urban cores of Houston (in 2014) and Detroit (in 2017). In general, most cities in the eastern U.S., except Pittsburgh, tended to stay in the same regime to which they were in 2011 or even came back to the  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime compared to the cities in the western U.S. Many recent studies have reported the changes in ozone chemical regime over the major U.S. metropolitan areas toward  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -limited regime in general, but with a narrow range in the reported year when the regime change occurred, and cities still remained in  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -limited condition, which is related to the dataset, study period, and methodology of each study; Chang et al. (2016) showed that ozone formation in Boston, Pittsburgh, Philadelphia, and Washington has changed to  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -limited or transition regimes in 2007-2014, while New York City mostly stayed in the transition regime (2007-2009) and VOC-limited regime (2007-2009 and 2014). Jin et al. (2020) addressed that  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated condition is occurring only in Los Angeles, Chicago, and New York City by 2013-2016, and Koplitz et al. (2021) showed regime change toward  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -limited condition over most of the U.S., and a few cities such as New York City, Chicago, Minneapolis, San Francisco, and Los Angeles are still remained in  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated regime as in 2016.\", \"In this study, we examined changes in the ozone chemical regime over the CONUS in the summers of 2011, 2014, and 2017, years in which U.S. NEI data were based. To combine the benefits of both satellite retrievals and CTMs, we employed an inverse modeling technique with OMI tropospheric  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO column retrievals to estimate  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions. To avoid misalignment of the emission adjustment, we applied dynamic chemical lateral boundary conditions and lightning-induced nitric oxide emissions, resulting in better representation of  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and  $\\\\mathrm{O}_3$  concentration in the upper atmosphere, confirmed by comparisons with measurements from the DISCOVER-AQ campaigns and two ozonesonde sites.\", \"Compared to OMI observations, the model with the top-down estimated  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  and NMVOCs emissions showed remarkably better performance for simulating tropospheric  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  and total HCHO columns and alleviated the nationwide underestimation of the  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  column and the overestimation of the HCHO column appearing over the southeastern U. S. We then used the CMAQ model to determine the threshold values of the ozone production regime, which closely represented the ozone chemical conditions of this study:  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -saturated (HCHO/  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2 < 1.6$ ), a transition  $(1.6 < \\\\mathrm{HCHO} / \\\\mathrm{NO}_2 < 2.6)$ , and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$ -limited (HCHO/  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2 > 2.6$ ), which is slightly distinct from other studies by Chang et al. (2016), Choi et al. (2012), Duncan et al. (2010) and Jin et al. (2017). This is because the threshold value of the HCHO/  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_2$  ratio represents the unique ozone chemistry of the CTM depending upon several influencing factors such as study domain, chemical mechanism, and meteorological condition; therefore, the applicability of the threshold value in other\", \"direct-sun DOAS technique: Intercomparisons and application to OMI validation.             Metadata: {"chunkid": "chunk_003109", "paperid": "paper_013", "type": "texture", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"MIX: a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission inventory under the international collaboration framework of the MICS-Asia and HTAP\", \"md_filename\": \"MIX_ a mosaic Asian anthropogenic emission invento.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 153, \"content_length\": 616, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "paper_id": "paper_013"}
            Content: {"text": "In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain."}

            Metadata: {"type": "texture", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission Characteristics from Different Types of Coking Plants in China\", \"md_filename\": \"Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission C.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 54, \"content_length\": 55, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "paperid": "paper_020", "chunkid": "chunk_004127", "paper_id": "paper_020"}
            Content: {"text": "# 3.1.1 Segment-based VOC emissions and source profiles"}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_004637
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"type": "picture", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission C\", \"figure_id\": \"img_193\", \"figure_title\": \"Fig. 1. Flow chart of coke production and VOCs emission sampling sites.\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Segment-based Volatile Organic Compound Emission C\\\\figure_1.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Methodology & Design\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Elucidate Model Architecture\", \"Illustrate Algorithm Process\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Diagrams & Schematics\", \"sub_types\": [\"Flowchart\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"coke production\", \"VOC emission sampling\", \"non-recovery coking plant\", \"mechanical coking plant\", \"stack flue gas\", \"thermal-recovery facility\", \"byproduct plant\"], \"concise_summary\": \"The figure illustrates the flow of coke production and indicates sites for sampling VOC emissions, comparing non-recovery with mechanical coking processes.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"The flowchart emphasizes the different emission segments and methods for VOC sampling in coke production, highlighting the impact of coking processes on VOC emissions.\", \"references\": [\"A flow chart of coke production and the VOC sampling sites (A–D) are shown in Fig. 1. The sites included stack flue gas from the non-recovery coking plant (SFN; Site A) and\", \"The source profiles, emission factors, and chemical reactivity of volatile organic compounds (VOCs) generated by two typical coking plants, one employing a non-recovery process and the other, a mechanical process, in Shanxi Province, a major coking hub in China, were determined for different segments of coke production during normal operations. The primary components in the stack flue gas were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, acetylene, and 2,2-dimethylbutane for the non-recovery plant and styrene, benzene, and ethylene for the mechanical plant. The fugitive emissions were also monitored at the mechanical plant, and the most abundant species leaking from the oven were benzene, toluene, ethane,  $m$ -xylene, and ethylene, whereas those leaking from byproducts were benzene, propane, ethane, ethylene,  $n$ -pentane,  $n$ -butane, isobutene, 1-butene, toluene, and propylene. The stack flue gas at the non-recovery and mechanical coking plants exhibited VOC emission factors of  $96 \\\\mathrm{~g} \\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4 \\\\mathrm{~g} \\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively; thus, VOCs released by the former merit greater concern. Since the highest ozone formation potential (OFP) was observed for the stack flue gas at the non-recovery plant ( $80.26 \\\\mathrm{mg} \\\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), followed by fugitive oven emissions at the mechanical plant ( $7.22 \\\\mathrm{mg} \\\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ ), controlling these VOCs will significantly reduce their conversion into ozone. Overall, replacing non-recovery coking plants with mechanical coking plants will decrease VOC emissions and improve the ambient air quality in China.\", \"implemented from 2008–2014, it still accounted for 8 million tons of coke in 2017 (CCIA, 2019). Mechanical coking plants produce coke in a carbonization chamber, then recover the byproducts from the coke oven gas, including tar, benzene, and ammonia. Differently, in non-recovery coking plants, the byproducts are burned and the heat is recovered during the coal conversion process (He, 2006). The emission pathways of pollutants vary with different coking process. Pollutants can be discharged into atmosphere from stack, coke oven leaks, or the byproduct production area in a mechanical coking plant. However, for non-recovery coking plants, pollutants are predominantly emitted from the stack; coke oven leaks can be neglected under the negative pressure conditions of the coke oven. Previous studies have found that pollutants such as particulate matter, nitrogen oxide, sulfur dioxide, toxic metals, polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), and volatile organic compounds (VOCs) are released into the environment during coke production (Mu et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2014; Mu et al., 2014). VOCs emitted from the coking process account for the largest proportion (12.8%) of all VOCs emitted from industrial processes (Wu et al., 2017). Long-term exposure to these pollutants, especially PAHs and VOCs, can lead to adverse health effects in coke plant workers (He et al., 2015).\", \"The source profiles and emission factors of VOCs are key information for establishing VOCs emission inventories, source apportionment, environmental management, etc. VOCs source profiles and emission factors have been widely researched in the USA, and the results have been integrated into the SPECIATE 4.5 database (U.S. EPA, 2016) and EPA AP-42 (U.S. EPA, 2008), respectively. The emission factors of various sources have also been identified in other countries, such as Denmark (EMEP, 2016) and UK (NAEI, 2018). Considering the large uncertainty inherent in referencing the results from other countries, scientists in China have recently obtained some VOCs' domestic source profiles and emission factors (Shi et al., 2015; Yan et al., 2016; Zhong et al., 2017; Shen et al., 2018; Yang et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2018; Zhong et al., 2018). For example, for the coking industry, He (2006) reported that C4 and C6 hydrocarbons are the most abundant VOCs in the coking oven stack flue gas from a coke plant, whereas C6 and C8 hydrocarbons are dominant in coal charging and coke discharging. By comprehensively evaluating the coking process and VOCs from the top of the coke oven, Li (2009) revealed that the major VOCs emissions from coke production are benzene, ethane, ethylene, propylene, toluene, propane, and 1-butene. Jia et al. (2009) identified the major VOCs species of coke oven gas as ethylene, ethane, propene, propane, benzene, toluene, and xylene, and showed that the concentration of VOCs from the coal charging process is significantly higher than that from coking. Moreover, Shi et al. (2015) showed that toluene and  $n$ -hexane are the most abundant species in coke production, whereas Li et al. (2018) found that the five most abundant species emitted from the coking process are acetylene, benzene, ethane, toluene, and propene, accounting for  $>86\\\\%$  of total VOCs. The source profiles of VOCs for the coking process differ between different studies. This suggests that VOCs from different emission segments or processes may affect the overall source profile for coke production.\", \"In terms of VOCs emission factors from Chinese coke production, He (2006) calculated a VOCs emission factor via stack flue gas of  $487.16\\\\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke. Emission factors of VOCs from coal charging  $(56.84\\\\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke) and coke discharging  $(1.26\\\\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke) have also been reported by Li (2009). In the emission factor guidebooks of U.S. EPA (2008), NAEI (2018), and EMEP (2016), the emission factors of VOCs were given as  $68\\\\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke,  $41\\\\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke, and  $47\\\\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  coal, respectively. This difference between VOC emission factors from different studies could lead to uncertainty in emission estimates. Moreover, previous source profiles only consider the coking process of mechanical coking plants, neglecting coke oven leaks and byproduct production as well as the coking process in non-recovery coking plants; therefore, the existing source profiles may not accurately reflect VOCs emissions from different coking segments. As such, it is crucial to determine localized VOCs source profiles and emission factors based on different emission segments within different types of coking plants in China, specifically, mechanical and nonrecovery coking plants.\", \"Two typical coking plants, a non-recovery coking plant and a mechanical coking plant, were selected to obtain the source profiles of VOCs from different emission segments. The coking plants locate in Shanxi Province, a centralized coking plant area in China. Designed annual yield of coke of non-recovery and mechanical coking plant is 0.5 million tons and 1.6 million tons with the operating load is  $75\\\\%$  and  $75\\\\%$  during the sampling time, respectively. The height of the coke oven in the non-recovery and mechanical coking plant is  $2.1\\\\mathrm{m}$  and  $4.3\\\\mathrm{m}$ , respectively. Detailed information of the coking plants is listed in Table 1. It is important to note the cleaned coal for coking is put into the furnace directly through a closed cleaned coal conveyance corridor in non-recovery coking plant. As the predominantly emitted pathway of pollutants in non-recovery coking plant, stack flue gas is the target in this study. While coke oven leakage is ignored due to the negative pressure conditions of the coke oven during the normal operation. Like most mechanical coking plants, metallurgical coke is produced in coke oven under the positive pressure condition and chemical products, including tar, benzene, and ammonium sulfate, come from coal gas recovery in this studied mechanical coking plant. VOCs can leak from coke oven and pipeline in these processes. Thus, the stack flue gas, oven leaks, and byproduct plant leaks are all investigated in the mechanical coking plant in this study. Samples were collected from the stacks after desulfurization and deducting.\", \"was collected after the byproducts burning and heat recovering in non-recovery coking plant, while different, it was gathered after byproducts recovery in mechanical coking plant. The samples were collected in Tedlar bags (with a volume of 2 L), which are made of Teflon and covered in black plastic. During sampling, the sampling bags were connected to a stainless-steel tube, whose length was enough sufficient to reach into the stack through the sampling holes, and a sampling pump (LP-4™; A.P. Buck Inc., USA), in which all interfaces were sealed. Samples were pumped into the sampling bags at the flow rate of  $1.0 \\\\, \\\\text{L} \\\\cdot \\\\text{min}^{-1}$  for a sampling duration of 2 min. To sample fugitive emissions, i.e., leaks, stainless-steel SUMMA canisters (1.0 L Siliconite® Canisters; Entech Instruments Inc., CA, USA), pre-cleaned with high-purity nitrogen and evacuated to vacuum then equipped with a restricted sampler (39-RS-0; Entech Instruments Inc., CA, USA) were used to collect the instantaneous samples for a sampling duration of 5 min. The sampling sites (Sites C-D) were located at the top of the coke oven according to the State Standard of the People's Republic of China (GB 16171-2012) and approximately 20 m downwind of the byproduct production workshop area, respectively. Parallel experiments were conducted whereby samples were collected from the same sampling point at least three times to ensure that the results were representative.\", \"Emission factors are used as essential data for building the emission inventories and for environmental management. Production engineering, equipment, fuel sources, combustion conditions, treatments, etc. are all factors influencing VOCs source emissions. The VOCs EFs for SFN and SFM were  $96\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke and  $0.4\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ -coke, respectively (Fig. 4), indicating that the emission intensity from the stack in a non-recovery coking plant is far greater than that in a mechanical coking plant. As the coke oven gas produced by the coking procedure in a non-recovery coking plant operates under negative pressure, the atmospheric pollutants are predominantly discharged through the stack to the atmosphere. However, the EFs of coke production from the mechanical coking plant only involved estimation of the VOCs emissions from the stack flue gas and neglected fugitive emissions such as coke oven and byproduct plant leaks, which partly explains the small EFs from the stack in the mechanical coking plant. As shown in Fig. 4, the EFs of alkenes and aromatics in SFN and SFM were  $67.15\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$  and  $0.29\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively, representing the groups with highest emission intensity. The major species with high emission intensity in SFN were ethylene, 1-butene, benzene, and acetylene, with EFs of  $40.58\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $25.26\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $11.71\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , and  $9.89\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively. For SFM, the major species were styrene, benzene, and ethylene, with EFs of  $0.16\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ ,  $0.08\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , and  $0.04\\\\mathrm{g}\\\\mathrm{Mg}^{-1}$ , respectively. The results of this study indicate that phasing out non-recovery coking plants in China and strongly promoting mechanical coking plants instead would help reduce VOCs emissions, especially alkene emissions, which is significant for improving the ambient air quality.\", \"A significant difference was observed in the EFs of the mechanical coking plant analyzed in this study and those reported in previous studies around the world, such as  $68\\\\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke in USA (U.S. EPA, 2008),  $41\\\\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  -coke in UK (NAEI, 2018), and  $47\\\\mathrm{gMg^{-1}}$  coal in Denmark (EMEP, 2016). Although the EFs in this study might be lower than the actual emissions because of the limited sampling set, VOCs emissions from the stack in a mechanical coking plant may be overestimated using previous emission factors. Many factors can contribute to this discrepancy, including coal quality, coking process, flue gas treatments, etc. The conversion efficiency from coal to coke varies with the coal quality in different countries, which would lead to the change of VOCs emissions from coking production in different countries. In addition, the emissions of VOCs decreased with the upgrading of coking process and the terminal control measures of flue gas implementing. That may also be the reasons of VOCs emission factors from stack in the studied mechanical coking plant is lower than that in the others results. In terms of the EFs from\", \"We measured the VOCs emitted from various segments of coke production at a non-recovery plant and a mechanical coking plant in China and then analyzed the source profiles, emission factors, and chemical reactivity. The VOCs were monitored in the stack flue gas of both the non-recovery (SFN) and the mechanical (SFM) plant as well as in fugitive emissions due to leakage\", \"He, Q., Yan, Y., Zhang, Y., Wang, X., Wang, Y. (2015). Coke workers' exposure to volatile organic compounds in northern China: A case study in Shanxi Province. Environ. Monit.             Content: {"text": "The HTAP_v2.2 air pollutant grid maps are considered to combine latest available regional information within a complete global data set. The disaggregation by sectors, high spatial and temporal resolution and detailed information on the data sources and references used will provide the user the required transparency. Because HTAP_v2.2 contains primarily official and/or widely used regional emission grid maps, it can be recommended as a global baseline emission inventory, which is regionally accepted as a reference and from which different scenarios assessing emission reduction policies at a global scale could start."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---

            Metadata: {"paperid": "paper_002", "type": "picture", "chunkid": "chunk_004630", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Quantifying on-road vehicle emissions during traff\", \"figure_id\": \"img_186\", \"figure_title\": \"Fig. 6. (a) Vehicle emission factors under different driving speeds. (b) Spatial distribution of hourly average on-road vehicle driving speeds (in km/h) during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time). (c) Spatial distribution of hourly average on-road vehicle driving speeds (in km/h) during off-peak hours (from 9:00 to 16:30 and from 18:30 to 7:00 local time). (d) Spatial distribution of hourly average traffic fluxes during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time). (e) Spatial distribution of hourly average traffic fluxes during off-peak hours (from 9:00 to 16:30 and from 18:30 to 7:00 local time).\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Quantifying on-road vehicle emissions during traff\\\\figure_6.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Results & Findings\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Present Core Findings\", \"Showcase Prediction Results\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Composite & Multi-view\", \"sub_types\": [\"Line Chart\", \"Map\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"Vehicle Emission Factors\", \"Driving Speeds\", \"Traffic Fluxes\", \"Xiaoshan District\", \"CO\", \"HC\", \"NOx\"], \"concise_summary\": \"The figure illustrates vehicle emission factors relative to driving speeds and spatial distribution of vehicle speeds and traffic fluxes in Xiaoshan District during different times, highlighting emission variations.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"Traffic congestion significantly impacts vehicle emissions, with higher emissions observed at lower speeds. The detailed visual data supports improved traffic management to reduce emissions in urban environments.\", \"references\": [\"Vehicle emissions vary with instantaneous driving conditions (Fu et al., 2012; Huang et al., 2020b; Mera et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2020). To investigate how vehicle emissions vary under the different working conditions, emission factors of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  were divided into 11 groups according to driving speeds. Slow driving conditions, normal urban driving conditions and high-speed driving conditions were defined as driving with speeds of  $0 - 5\\\\mathrm{km / h}$ ,  $30 - 35\\\\mathrm{km / h}$  and larger than  $50\\\\mathrm{km / h}$ , respectively (Feng et al., 2020; Fu et al., 2012; Kumar et al., 2015). As presented in Fig. 6(a), unexpected high emissions under the idling conditions were found in vehicles of all emission standards. Emission factors of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  decreased sharply with the increase of vehicle speeds. The emission factors of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  were the highest under the slow driving conditions, with average values of 8.35, 0.44, and  $0.71\\\\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. When speeds were lower than  $5\\\\mathrm{km / h}$ , the average emission factors of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  were extremely high under the normal urban driving conditions with the values of 1.48, 0.07, and  $0.19\\\\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. Under the high-speed driving conditions, the average emission factors of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  were 1.01, 0.05, and  $0.14\\\\mathrm{g / km}$ , respectively. In summary, the emission factors of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  under the slow driving conditions were 5-9 times higher than under the high-speed conditions ( $>50\\\\mathrm{km / h}$ ) and 3-6 times higher than under the normal urban driving conditions. Emission factors under the slow driving conditions were significantly higher than previous studies (Huang et al., 2020b; Yang et al., 2020b. One of the possible reasons might be that the emission data of second by second used in this study can better capture the ultra-high emissions at low speeds or even idling speeds. In contrast with the significant\", \"Traffic congestion frequently occurs due to the rapid increases in vehicle population, especially in megacities. On this occasion, vehicles may leave the engine idling for several minutes or longer, which leads to massive emissions (Feng et al., 2020; Sentoff et al., 2010; Shancita et al., 2014; Zhang et al., 2020). Lab measurements showed that the low-speed vehicles produced much higher emissions. Real-world traffic observations allowed us to know where the low-speed motor vehicles were usually distributed and how many emissions they produced. On-road vehicle emissions under the low-speed driving and high-speed driving conditions can be further quantified so as to reveal the significant impact of traffic congestion on the urban vehicle emissions, which so far has no observational evidence. As shown in Fig. 6(b-c), observed traffic congested roads were widespread, mainly distributed in the Airport Highway and the arterial roads. These road segments accounted for around  $20\\\\%$  of the whole roads.\", \"Xiaoshan District is at the forefront of the country in the digital transformation and upgrading of the government. It is one of the few areas that have achieved full coverage of integrated traffic monitoring, and has been interconnected through its intelligent transportation system (called \\\"city brain\\\") since 2017 (Hua, 2018). This provide the opportunity to obtain detailed road traffic and road network information. According to 2021 China Urban Transportation Report released by Baidu Map (Baidu Map, 2021), the congestion index during rush hours of Hangzhou ranks top 9 among cities with  $>3$  million motor vehicles. As the transportation hub of Hangzhou, Xiaoshan District, with dense road network, its speed and traffic flow distribution characteristics can well represent the traffic conditions of large cities. Equipped with real-time online traffic monitoring facilities, the core road network in Xiaoshan District is divided into 4585 road segments. Traffic video records, together with image recognition algorithms (Redmon et al., 2016), were applied to detect vehicle license plates and thus monitor traffic fluxes, vehicle categories and driving speeds. These license plate recognition (LPR) data include the records of traffic fluxes and vehicle-specific speeds of on-road vehicles in Xiaoshan District. The average daily detection records were 4.23 million. As a result, we constructed a dataset of total  $1.53 \\\\times 10^{9}$  records from 1 January 2021 to 31 December 2021.\", \"A hyperfine-resolution bottom-up model was used to calculate the on-road vehicle emissions in Xiaoshan District, following the method of Jiang et al. (2021). Hourly observations of surface temperature and humidity were used to reflect local meteorological conditions. Meteorological observations came from two sets of real-time online remote sensing monitoring equipment in Xiaoshan District. The information of these remote sensing equipment was introduced in detail in our previous study (Xia et al., 2022). Real-time emissions were quantified on the basis of the vehicle emission factors, driving speeds, traffic fluxes, and the length of road segments:\", \"Fig. 4. Hourly average on-road vehicle emission intensities of the  $10\\\\%$  high emitters under different congestion scenarios during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time).\", \"from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time) and off-peak hours (from 9:00 to 16:30 and from 18:30 to 7:00 local time), respectively. On this basis, two smooth traffic (all speeds  $>25\\\\mathrm{km / h}$ ) scenarios (S1 and S4) and two severe congested scenarios (S3 and S6) were designed to represent different traffic conditions. Fleet composition scenario F0 was designed based on the local official vehicle Inspect/Maintenance (I/M) dataset, representing the current composition of road vehicles. On this basis, F1 and F2 scenarios were designed by considering the upgrading of emission standards and the increasing proportion of electric vehicles, respectively. Combining traffic congestion scenarios S1-S6 and fleet composition scenarios F0-F3, a total of 18 scenarios were studied (Table 2 and Table 3). For example, scenario S2F0 quantified the vehicle emissions when the traffic condition is S2 and the fleet composition is F0, which represents the current rush-hour traffic condition and current on-road fleet compositions. Similarly, scenario S5F0 quantified the vehicle emissions when the traffic condition is S5 and the fleet composition is F0, which represents the current traffic condition during off-peak hours and current on-road fleet compositions. In the subsequent comparison, scenarios S2F0 and S5F0 were used as the benchmark scenarios for rush hour and off-peak hours, respectively. Overall, our results show that under the ideal situations of no traffic congestion (i.e., S1 and S4), the emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  will be significantly reduced with the continuous upgrading of fleet composition (from F0 to F2). However, the impacts of traffic\", \"Fig. 7. Hourly average on-road vehicle emission intensities under different congestion scenarios during rush hours (from 07:00 to 09:00 and from 16:30 to 18:30 local time).\", \"standard upgrading, and even increased on-road vehicle emissions instead (see S1F2, S2F2, S3F2 in Table 2). Similarly, if the traffic congestion were effectively alleviated (S4F0) during the off-peak hours, the hourly emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\\\mathrm{NO}_{\\\\mathrm{x}}$  could be reduced by  $-14.2\\\\%$ ,  $-21.4\\\\%$ , and  $-9.6\\\\%$  respectively, but increased by  $7.8\\\\%$ ,  $11.4\\\\%$ , and  $5.2\\\\%$  if the congestions were aggravated (S6F0) (Figs. S11-S13, Table 3). Note that, in the southern areas of the district, the emission intensities in the arterial and residential streets far exceeded ( $\\\\sim 5$  times) those in the northern areas (Fig. 7, Fig. S11). These high emission hotspots were mainly attributed to the high traffic fluxes and low traffic speeds. Frequent traffic congestion, accelerations and decelerations led to significant increases in vehicles' fuel consumption. Thus, better traffic management policies should be applied to reduce vehicles' energy consumption and mitigate pollutant emissions as well (Huang et al., 2020b).\", \"Huang, W., Guo, Y., Xu, X., 2020b.         You are an **Evaluator** in a scientific literature investigation team.

        If an image is available, include visual judgment.

           - **GROW**: high-value evidence, strongly relevant, with clear follow-up clues.

            Metadata: {"metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga\", \"figure_id\": \"img_148\", \"figure_title\": \"Fig. 2. Concentrations of VOC components during dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga\\\\figure_2.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Results & Findings\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Present Quantitative Results\", \"Present Core Findings\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Quantitative Charts\", \"sub_types\": [\"Bar Chart\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"VOC components\", \"OVOCs\", \"aromatic hydrocarbons\", \"biogenic species\", \"tracers\"], \"concise_summary\": \"Figure 2 displays the concentrations of VOC components in dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019, highlighting the dominance of OVOCs and the seasonal variations in VOC proportions.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"The figure illustrates the significant seasonal variation in VOC concentrations with OVOCs being the most prevalent, underscoring their role in atmospheric chemistry and the impact of pollution control measures.\", \"references\": [\"A total of 17 species of VOCs were monitored in this study by PTR-MS from 2014 to 2019, including 6 species classified as OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, formic acid, acetic acid, and MEK); 5 aromatic hydrocarbons (benzene, toluene, styrene, C8 and C9 aromatics); 3 classified as biogenic species (isoprene, MVK + MACR, and monoterpenes); and 2 species that, in addition to benzene, were measured as tracers (acetonitrile and DMS). The compounds, the respective  $\\\\mathrm{m} / \\\\mathrm{z}$  of their parent ion signal, and their detection limits are provided in Table S3. As shown in Fig. 2, the average of the sum of the VOC concentrations in the dry season over the six years ranged from 25.3 to 41.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 33.1 ppbv; for the wet season, the average value ranged from 23.0 to 39.0 ppbv, with an overall 6-year average of 29.4 ppbv. In terms of the proportion of each component, in the six years of dry and wet seasons, OVOCs (70–78%) accounted for the largest proportion, followed by aromatic hydrocarbons (16–26%), whereas the concentrations of biogenic VOCs (BVOCs) and tracers were relatively low (less than 10%). Figure S6 shows the time series of 17 species of VOCs during the sampling period. To evaluate the concentrations of OVOCs in this study, comparative results of the concentrations of six OVOCs in five urban areas in China are listed in Table 1. Compared to Guangzhou and Hong Kong, two other mega-cities in the PRD region, the concentrations of acetaldehyde, acetone, and MEK in Shenzhen were higher than those in Hong\", \"Oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) are important precursors and intermediate products of atmospheric photochemical reactions, which can promote the formation of secondary pollutants such as ozone  $(\\\\mathrm{O}_3)$  and secondary organic aerosol (SOA). However, there have been few studies on the sources of and long-term variation in ambient OVOCs. This study combined sensitive, near real-time measurements of VOCs by proton transfer reaction-mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) with an improved photochemical age parameterization method to quantify daytime sources of OVOCs in an urban atmosphere in China from 2014 to 2019, permitting the observation of the impacts of emission control strategies that were implemented during this period. Temporal variation in six key OVOCs (methanol, acetaldehyde, acetone, methyl ethyl ketone (MEK), formic acid, and acetic acid) were observed. The sum of concentrations of OVOCs was averagely  $13\\\\%$  higher during the dry season (November to April), when winds transported polluted air masses to Shenzhen from the continent, than during the wet season, and peak diurnal levels occurred during the daytime year-round due to photochemical production and higher daytime anthropogenic emissions. The average dry season concentration of OVOCs declined from a peak of 30.3 ppb in 2015 to 18.7 ppb in 2019. The results of source apportionment showed that primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to methanol, MEK, and acetic acid  $(32 - 51\\\\%)$ ; the dominant sources of acetaldehyde and formic acid were both primary and secondary anthropogenic sources; and biomass burning contributed a small fraction  $(5 - 11\\\\%)$  to the six OVOCs. From 2014 to 2019, contributions from primary anthropogenic sources of OVOCs decreased significantly by  $50 - 60\\\\%$  due to intensive pollution control measures in Shenzhen, whereas pollution control measures had no observable impact on secondary OVOCs, indicating their formation was not limited by availability of their primary VOC precursors.\", \"As presented in Fig. 1, Shenzhen is located in the PRD of Guangdong Province, China. The sampling site for this study was located on the campus of the Shenzhen Graduate School of Peking University (PKUSZ,  $22.60^{\\\\circ}\\\\mathrm{N}$ ,  $113.97^{\\\\circ}\\\\mathrm{E}$ ) in western Shenzhen, which is predominantly surrounded by green areas, teaching buildings and apartments. No evident anthropogenic emission sources of air pollutants surrounded the sampling site, except a highway approximately  $100\\\\mathrm{m}$  away. Previous studies have shown that the concentrations of air pollutants such as  $\\\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  and VOCs at this site were approximately equal to their average concentrations in the entire PRD region (Louie et al., 2013; Huang et al., 2018). Therefore, this site was considered suitable for long-term observation of trends in air pollutants in the PRD region. Table S1 summarizes the effective days of VOCs observed in each month from 2014 to 2019, with 1169 days in total over the six years of sampling. During the six-year sampling period, the loss of data was mainly due to instrument failure and bad weather, especially in the wet season due to frequent rainfall, resulting in a higher rate of missing data during\", \"Shenzhen belongs to the subtropical marine monsoon climate zone. Marked differences in the four seasons were not evident, and the observation time each year was slightly different; therefore, this study divided the entire year into a dry season and a wet season according to the monthly temperature and precipitation. As presented in Figure S1, the period from May to October, with higher temperatures and abundant precipitation, was defined as the wet season, and the period from November to April, with less precipitation, was defined as the dry season. The meteorological data are from the Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality (Meteorological Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). In addition to the differences in temperature and precipitation, the sources of air masses in Shenzhen during the wet and dry seasons were also very different. Taking January and July 2018 as an example, the 48-h backward trajectories at  $100\\\\mathrm{m}$  above ground level in Shenzhen  $(113.9^{\\\\circ}\\\\mathrm{E}, 22.6^{\\\\circ}\\\\mathrm{N})$  were calculated beginning at 12:00 UTC using the Hybrid Single Particle Lagrangian Integrated Trajectory (HYSPLIT) model (Draxler and Rolph, 2003). The results show that the origin of the air mass passing through Shenzhen in different seasons was very different: during the dry season, the air mass mainly came from the northern mainland, and during the wet season, it mainly came from the South China Sea, which has also been explained in detail in our previous studies (Zou et al., 2017). In addition, referencing existing studies of air pollution in the Pearl River Delta, many scholars have adopted a method of dividing the wet and dry seasons (Cui et al., 2015). Figure S2 shows the changes in major meteorological parameters during the six-year observation period in Shenzhen. Analysis of major meteorological parameters in Shenzhen (Fig S2) did not indicate significant interannual variability in temperature, rainfall, humidity or wind conditions that could have substantially influenced the long-term variation in atmospheric pollutants observed in this study.\", \"It should be emphasized that because the sampling data of this study span six years, in addition to the above assumptions, one more assumption should be added: (4) during the sampling period, there was no significant change in the ratio of OVOC anthropogenic emissions to tracers (benzene) in Shenzhen. The emission reduction strategy of Shenzhen is to reduce emission sources of pollutants as a whole, such as replacing gasoline vehicles with electric vehicles and closing down or moving VOC-emitting enterprises to other areas. These measures have reduced emissions of VOCs, but the emission ratio among specific VOC species is relatively stable. Toluene/benzene (T/B) was considered a good indicator to judge the contribution of motor vehicle sources and solvent use to VOC emissions (Nelson and Quigley, 1984; Wang et al., 2002; Araizaga et al., 2013). Figure S5 shows the changes in the concentrations of benzene and toluene and T/B from 2014 to 2019. The concentrations of benzene and toluene in the six years showed a downward trend, while T/B remained at approximately 5, which also indicated\", \"In this study, the six-year average of VOCs in the dry season was approximately  $13\\\\%$  higher than that in the wet season. As shown in Figure S7, the concentrations of OVCs, aromatic hydrocarbons and tracers in the wet season were lower than those in the dry season, while the BVOC concentration was higher in the wet season. As a highly developed urban area, Shenzhen was active in anthropogenic activities year-round, and there was no obvious seasonal difference in anthropogenic emissions. Therefore, it is more likely that seasonal meteorological differences lead to variations in VOC concentrations between wet and dry seasons. For example, the air masses passing through Shenzhen during the dry season were predominantly from northern inland regions, and the concentration of pollutants carried by inland air masses was higher than that of marine air masses (He et al., 2011; Huang et al., 2020). In addition, the dry season had a lower boundary layer and less precipitation, which was also conducive to the accumulation of pollutants. However, the concentration of BVOCs during the wet season was significantly higher than during the dry season, as shown in Figure S7 (g), (h) and (i), primarily because the higher light levels and solar radiation during the wet season led to accelerated release rates of VOCs by plants (Loreto et al., 2010). It should be pointed out that the data collection frequency in the dry season  $(\\\\sim 73\\\\%)$  was much higher than that in the wet season  $(\\\\sim 35\\\\%)$ , which may lead to relatively higher uncertainty of the conclusion for the wet season.\", \"The average diurnal variations in mixing ratios of  $\\\\mathrm{O_3}$  and selected VOCs during the dry and wet seasons are shown in Fig. 4. To more clearly compare the diurnal variation trends of the pollutants, all concentrations were normalized (divided by their respective average concentrations). The diurnal variations of the same species during the dry and wet seasons were similar, but  $\\\\mathrm{O_3}$  and OVOCs rose relatively more during the daytime during the warmer wet season. The diurnal variation of benzene showed two peaks corresponding to the morning and afternoon traffic emissions, but the concentration decreased in the afternoon, which may\", \"be due to the dilution caused by the uplift of the planetary boundary layer (PBL). However, the six OVOCs exhibited a significant upward trend during the daytime. These results suggested that photochemical production and/or daytime anthropogenic activities related to OVC emissions were notable and could overwhelm photochemical loss. The diurnal variation trend of acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\\\\mathrm{O_3}$ , exhibiting a greater difference in concentration between day and night during the wet season, indicating that the majority of acetaldehyde originated from daytime secondary generation (Chang et al., 2019). The daytime splits of the diurnal curves of methanol, MEK, and acetic acid, similar to benzene, implied that they could have relatively larger contributions from primary emissions sources, such as motor vehicle emissions. (Guo et al., 2011, Zheng et al., 2013). The diurnal variations in VOCs in our previous two individual campaigns, which were carried out during the dry season (Huang et al., 2019, 2020), were almost the same as those in the dry season in this study.\", \"Fig. 4. Normalized average diurnal variations in  $\\\\mathrm{O_3}$  and typical VOCs during the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019 (shaded part:  $\\\\pm \\\\mathrm{SD}$ ).\", \"Fig. 5. The average mixing ratios of the measured VOCs and calculated OFPs in the dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019.\", \"The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\\\\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\\\\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\\\\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\\\\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\\\\%)$\", \"Fig. 6 shows the trends in the relative contributions to OVOCs from 2014 to 2019 during the dry and wet seasons. In general, during the six years from 2014 to 2019, primary anthropogenic sources contributed the most to OVOCs during Shenzhen's dry season, but the proportion exhibited a decreasing trend year over year, and the proportion during the six-year period decreased by approximately  $15\\\\%$  overall. This continuous decreasing trend indicates that the VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas, where most of the industry and traffic of Shenzhen is located, had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. The OVOC source compositions during the wet season, when clean marine air masses from the south dominated the sampling site, did not exhibit a clear year-by-year trend. The contributions of secondary anthropogenic sources to OVOC concentrations (except that of methanol) during the wet season were significantly higher (approximately 1.3-1.6 times greater) than those during the dry season, indicating that secondary transformation of NMHCs during the wet season contributed greatly to OVOC concentrations. Biogenic sources reasonably contributed a larger proportion of OVOC concentrations during the wet season, whereas biomass burning contributed a larger fraction of acetaldehyde during the dry season.\", \"hydrocarbon and 490 tons of NOx. VOC emission reduction in industrial enterprises and the coating industry occurs mainly through upgrading emission standards and closing down industries with high VOC emissions. For example, furniture manufacturing used to be an important industry in Shenzhen but also a key VOC-emitting industry. From 2010 to 2016, 565 coating production lines were removed or relocated in Shenzhen, reducing VOC emissions by more than 30,000 tons every year. Since 2017, Shenzhen has completely banned solvent paints and adhesives in the manufacturing process and replaced them with water-based paints to further reduce VOC emissions, especially the emissions of aldehydes and ketones commonly used in the production of furniture manufacturing (Ecological Environment Bureau of Shenzhen Municipality, 2019). As a commonly used tracer of anthropogenic sources of VOCs, the annual average concentration of benzene during the dry season from 2014 to 2019 was 0.98, 0.81, 0.64, 0.61, 0.49, and 0.45 ppb (Figure S5), respectively, reflecting the decreasing trend in primary VOC emissions in recent years. Therefore, the decrease in OVOC concentrations during the dry season over the six-year study period could primarily be attributed to decreases in the contributions from primary anthropogenic sources. However, the contribution of secondary anthropogenic\", \"Fig. 7. Change in ambient concentrations of OVOCs contributed by different sources during the dry seasons of 2014-2019.\", \"PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\\\\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\\\\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\\\\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\\\\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\\\\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\\\\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\\\\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.\", \"Understanding primary and secondary sources of ambient carbonyl compounds in Beijing using the PMF model. Atmospheric Pollution Research 8 (6), 1193-1202. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apr.2017.05.001.\"], \"chunk_type\": \"figure\"}", "chunkid": "chunk_004592", "paperid": "paper_014", "type": "picture", "paper_id": "paper_014"}
            Content: {"text": "Figure 2 displays the concentrations of VOC components in dry and wet seasons from 2014 to 2019, highlighting the dominance of OVOCs and the seasonal variations in VOC proportions. The figure illustrates the significant seasonal variation in VOC concentrations with OVOCs being the most prevalent, underscoring their role in atmospheric chemistry and the impact of pollution control measures."}

            ID: chunk_004649
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"chunkid": "chunk_004649", "type": "picture", "paperid": "paper_006", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\", \"figure_id\": \"img_205\", \"figure_title\": \"Fig. 8. Scatter plots between the source profiles derived from the measured and initial VOC levels. The error bars represent the standard deviations of the bootstrap source profiles  N = 100 . The color solid lines represent the linear regression results and the filled ribbons represent the  95\\\\%  confidence intervals of fitting. The black solid lines represent the 1:1 line.\", \"save_path\": \"C:\\\\liuxingyu\\\\multisubspace-data\\\\predataprocess\\\\paper_md\\\\Source apportionment of volatile organic compounds\\\\figure_8.jpg\", \"functional_roles\": {\"main_role\": \"Results & Findings\", \"sub_roles\": [\"Present Core Findings\", \"Compare Experimental Performance\"]}, \"visual_type\": {\"main_type\": \"Quantitative Charts\", \"sub_types\": [\"Scatter Plot\"]}, \"key_entities\": [\"source profiles\", \"VOC levels\", \"PMF model\", \"gasoline evaporation\", \"solvent usage\", \"combustion sources\", \"industrial sources\"], \"concise_summary\": \"The figure displays scatter plots comparing source profiles from measured and initial VOC levels. Linear regressions and confidence intervals are shown, highlighting variability and correlations in source profiles.\", \"inferred_insight\": \"The figure illustrates strong correlations for solvent usage and gasoline evaporation, but discrepancies in vehicle emissions suggest variability in photochemical processes impacting VOC profiles.\", \"references\": [\"results by the PMF modelling. The measured and initial datasets were both introduced into the PMF model and the source profiles (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3) from two datasets were compared as shown in Fig. 8. The source profiles of gasoline evaporation, solvent usage, combustion sources, and industrial sources derived from two different datasets were in line with each other, with Pearson coefficient  $(r^2)$  of 0.99, 0.85, 0.68, and 0.40, respectively. However, a great discrepancy was found for vehicle emissions. The PMF algorithms aim to find the profiles and contributions for a given number of factors that have the minimum weighed residues of the input dataset. If the importance of atmospheric photochemical processes exceeds the differences of emission profiles from various sources, the factors in PMF can still be identified according to the different degrees of photochemical processing (Yuan et al., 2012). The source profiles derived from the two datasets suggested that the importance of photochemistry in the atmosphere exceeded the differences in the VOC compositions of vehicle emissions. Compared to the source apportionment from the measured dataset, the contributions of solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources to total VOC mixing ratio derived from the initial dataset were higher, while vehicle emissions and combustion sources were lower (Fig. S4). The differences suggested that the VOC decay should be considered before source apportionment using the receptor model.\"], \"chunk_type\": \"figure\"}", "paper_id": "paper_006"}

        # Mission Overview
        You are the **Chief Scientist** in a scientific discovery team.
        Your team's goal is to investigate literature deeply and broadly, starting from the user's question, and discover new, valuable, and verifiable scientific knowledge.
        You are building a **research evidence tree** from retrieved and reviewed papers.

        # Team Roles
        You lead the direction of retrieval.
        Evaluators execute your retrieval plans, inspect text and figures, summarize findings, and assess whether each result should be expanded.
        Thinkers later synthesize validated evidence into a final scientific report.

        # Your Core Responsibilities
        1. **Initial planning**: for a new user question, propose multiple (3-5) search angles.
        2. **Dynamic decisions**: adapt based on evaluator feedback.
        3. **Direction control**: balance **breadth** (new directions) and **depth** (evidence-chain validation).

        # Decision Rules
        - When continuing retrieval, output a JSON list of strategy objects.
        - Each strategy object must have:
          - `action`: fixed as `call_tool`
          - `ParentNode`: evidence node ID (e.g., `chunk_xxx`) or `"0"` for global planning
          - `tool_name`: usually `strategy_semantic_search`, `strategy_metadata_search` or `strategy_exact_search`
          - `args`: tool arguments
          - `reason`: concise rationale

        ## Strategy Types
        1. **ParentNode Selection**:
           - Set to `"0"` for **BROADER (Exploration)**: discovering new directions when results are repetitive or sparse.
           - Set to a specific node ID (e.g., `"chunk_xxx"`) for **DEEPER (Exploitation)**: following up on a promising node to build an evidence chain.

        2. **Search Methods**:
           - `strategy_semantic_search`: Used for semantic similarity retrieval. Parameter: `query_intent` (a natural language phrase or sentence describing what you are looking for).
           - `strategy_metadata_search`: Used for retrieving a specific paper to explore its context. Parameter: `paper_id` (the ID of the paper).
           - `strategy_exact_search`: Used for exact text matching in the database. Parameter: `query_intent`. **IMPORTANT**: For exact search, you MUST use ONLY one or two specific proper nouns (e.g. "PM2.5" or "CNN-LSTM") rather than long phrases or sentences to prevent getting zero results. You should heavily refer to the `suggested_keywords` provided in the evaluation feedback.

        Before outputting each strategy, internally check:
        - Relevance to the original user question (0-10)
        - Novelty vs historical strategies (0-10)
        Only output if both are >= 7.

        # Historical Strategies
        Use historical strategies to avoid duplication and to improve novelty.

                        Node content: {'text': 'Many methods for allocating regional emission totals to grids are available. The most accurate approach involves allocating emissions for which the actual latitude-longitude coordinates of the emitting facilities (e.g., power plants or cement plants) are available. For mobile and area sources (i.e., sources for which the exact emission locations are unknown), parameters of so-called spatial proxies must be used to represent the spatial distributions of emissions. For example, emissions from road transportation sources can be allocated based on road networks, and residential emissions that are strongly related to human activities can be gridded using population densities or nighttime lights (Streets et al., 2003; Woo et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Zhang et al., 2009; Oda et al., 2011). In many cases, industrial emissions are also allocated by proxies because of the limited information available.'}. Paper ID: paper_022. Keywords: None

                        Node content: {'text': 'The resulting data set, named HTAP_v2.2, is a compilation of annual and monthly grid maps of anthropogenic air pollution emissions (with a  $0.1^{\\circ} \\times 0.1^{\\circ}$  grid resolution). It contains region-specific information on human activity (concerning intensity and geospatial distribution) and on fuel, technology- and process-dependent emission factors and end-of-pipe abatement, but it is not as consistent as a globally consistent emission inventory using international statistics and global geospatial distributions. With the perspective of being used in chemical transport models, this inventory includes the atmospheric gaseous pollutants  $\\mathrm{(SO_2, NO_x, CO, NMVOC^4, NH_3)}$  and particulate matter with carbonaceous speciation  $\\mathrm{(PM_{10}, PM_{2.5}, BC}$  and OC)5.'}.                         Node content: {'text': 'In the MIX inventory, gridded emissions for 10 gaseous and aerosol species were developed at  $0.25 \\times 0.25^{\\circ}$  resolution. Emission maps of all species in 2010 are shown in Fig. 12. Compared to the previous gridded Asian emission inventories, we believe the spatial patterns are improved because several local high-resolution emission datasets are incorporated, such as CPED for China and JEI-DB and OPRF for Japan. However, for sectors in which emissions are dominated by spatially scattered sources (e.g., residential combustion, solvent use), the spatial distributions in emissions are still uncertain.'}.
