            Metadata: {"chunkid": "chunk_002913", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) in an urban atmosphere in southern China, 2014-2019\\\\*\", \"md_filename\": \"Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 41, \"content_length\": 664, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "type": "texture", "paperid": "paper_014", "paper_id": "paper_014"}
            Content: {"text": "photochemical aging developed by de Gouw et al. (2005) has the advantage of not requiring emission inventories and considers the photochemical reactions and consumption processes of OVOCs (Han et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019, 2020). OVOC concentrations are attributed to primary anthropogenic sources, secondary anthropogenic sources, biogenic sources and the regional-scale background concentrations as described in equation (1), originally proposed by de Gouw et al. (2005), modified here with the addition of terms  $m$  and  $ER_{bb} * [Tracer_{bb}]$  as used in previous studies in this region (Wang et al., 2017, Han et al., 2019; Huang et al., 2019; 2020):"}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_002912
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) in an urban atmosphere in southern China, 2014-2019\\\\*\", \"md_filename\": \"Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 40, \"content_length\": 430, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "paperid": "paper_014", "type": "texture", "chunkid": "chunk_002912", "paper_id": "paper_014"}
            Content: {"text": "In previous studies, the source apportionment methods for OVOCs primarily included the multilinear regression method (Garcia et al., 2006), the chemical mass balance (CMB) model (Liu et al., 2015), the photochemical aging parameterization method (de Gouw et al., 2005; Huang et al., 2019) and the PMF model (Lau et al., 2010; Chen et al., 2014; Zhou et al., 2019). Compared with other methods, the parameterization method based on"}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_002916
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) in an urban atmosphere in southern China, 2014-2019\\\\*\", \"md_filename\": \"Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 44, \"content_length\": 775, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "chunkid": "chunk_002916", "paperid": "paper_014", "type": "texture", "paper_id": "paper_014"}
            Content: {"text": "When using photochemical-age-based parametric methods to analyze the sources of OVOCs, de Gouw et al. (2005) proposed the following assumptions: (1) anthropogenic emissions of OVOCs and their precursors are in direct proportion to the selected tracers of primary emissions; (2) OVOCs are mainly scavenged by reactions with OH radicals during the daytime; and (3) the biogenic source of OVOCs is proportional to the emission of isoprene. Therefore, the uncertainty of the method mainly depends on the effectiveness of the selected tracer, the assumed stable emission ratio and the simplified variability of VOC properties (de Gouw et al., 2005). In this study, some modifications were made to the equation based on the above assumptions to make the model more suitable for the"}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_002903
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"chunkid": "chunk_002903", "type": "texture", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile organic compounds (OVOCs) in an urban atmosphere in southern China, 2014-2019\\\\*\", \"md_filename\": \"Long-term observations of oxygenated volatile orga.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 25, \"content_length\": 2154, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "paperid": "paper_014", "paper_id": "paper_014"}
            Content: {"text": "Previous studies have proven that some low-carbon OVOCs (formaldehyde and acetaldehyde) can generate OH radicals and hydroperoxyl radicals  $\\mathrm{(HO_2)}$  through photolysis to affect the oxidizing capacity of the atmosphere (Volkamer et al., 2010; Shao et al., 2011; Xue et al., 2016). OVOCs can also participate in photochemical reactions and contribute to the generation of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  and SOA (Louie et al., 2013; Ehn et al., 2014). In addition, as most OVOCs are highly water soluble, they can be absorbed into the human body, irritating the eyes, skin and respiratory tract, thus harming human health. OVOCs such as formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acetone and acrolein have been shown to pose serious health risks (WHO, 2000, Delfino et al., 2003; Bashkin, 2009; Du et al., 2014). Given the important role of OVOCs in photochemical reactions and the complexity of their sources, OVOCs have been the subject of extensive research recently (Sahu et al., 2017; Maji et al., 2020), especially in China, where air pollution is severe (Zhang et al., 2012, 2015; Zhou et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Zhang et al. (2012) studied the pollution characteristics of BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, m,p-xylene and o-xylene) and carbonyl compounds during summer in Beijing and found that the  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  formation potential of carbonyl compounds  $(166.1~\\mu \\mathrm{g} / \\mathrm{m}^3)$  was approximately 2.5 times that of BTEX  $(65.4~\\mu \\mathrm{g} / \\mathrm{m}^3)$ . Zhou et al. (2019) used proton transfer reaction mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) to measure VOCs in Xi'an and used the positive matrix factorization (PMF) model to analyze over 30 types of VOCs and identify eight primary sources and found that local emissions, such as industrial processes, solvent usage, residential biofuel use and vehicular exhaust, contributed about  $70\\%$  of the VOCs measured. Zhu et al. (2019) used PTR-MS to monitor OVOCs during different seasons and applied photochemical age parameter analysis to evaluate the sources of OVOCs in Shenzhen. They found that primary anthropogenic sources were the most important source of OVOCs in urban areas of China."}
            

```

            Content: {"text": "![](https://cdn-mineru.openxlab.org.cn/result/2025-12-03/e2deac70-4266-4e5d-a059-def38bf66669/e30ed81d7db5af4386f9d8c296676efdb52aa1e59dfd35a85727667163d56464.jpg)\nFig. 6. Emission factors of 21 inorganic elements in  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$ ."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_003220
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Mobile measurement of vehicle emission factors in a roadway tunnel: A concentration gradient approach\", \"md_filename\": \"Mobile measurement of vehicle emission factors in .md\", \"paragraph_index\": 57, \"content_length\": 1471, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "paperid": "paper_027", "chunkid": "chunk_003220", "type": "texture", "paper_id": "paper_027"}
            Content: {"text": "The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations were in the ranges of 41.0-87.5 and  $23.5 - 41.3\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively. The PM concentrations varied substantially compared with the other pollutants (Fig. 2g and h). The maximum  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  concentration  $(188\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  was observed when the mobile laboratory passed next to a HDDV, indicating that PM can be strongly affected by the number of HDDVs on the road, which accounted for a relatively high proportion  $(9\\%)$  of the traffic volume in the SJT. Non-combustion PM sources in the road environment include tire wear, brake abrasion, and resuspended dust (Abu-Allaban et al., 2003). Because photochemical reactions are rare in tunnels, the main contributors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were primary sources, i.e., elemental carbon and primary organic aerosols from vehicle emissions and small particles from non-combustion emissions. The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5} / \\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ratio remained relatively constant during the campaign (approximately 0.5), indicative of the mixed effects of vehicle combustion and non-combustion sources. Therefore, the intermittent increases in the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  concentration due to HDDVs was likely caused by tire-road interactions (such as the resuspension of road dust, tire wear, and other factors). The concentration gradients and detailed statistical information are presented in Table S3."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_002887
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)

            Content: {"text": "emissions and their relationships to local parameters can be obtained, which has implications for the local selection of spatial proxies."}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_002796
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)

            Content: {"text": "# Impact of spatial proxies on the representation of bottom-up emission inventories: A satellite-based analysis"}
            

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_002823
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Impact of spatial proxies on the representation of bottom-up emission inventories: A satellite-based analysis\", \"md_filename\": \"Impact of spatial proxies on the representation of.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 30, \"content_length\": 791, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "type": "texture", "paperid": "paper_022", "chunkid": "chunk_002823", "paper_id": "paper_022"}
            Content: {"text": "We then develop six sets of gridded  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission data using the same magnitude of emissions from the MEIC and different spatial proxies, as presented in Table 3. Many geographic information system (GIS) grid-based spatial proxies (e.g., population density and road networks) are at a resolution of  $1\\mathrm{km}\\times 1\\mathrm{km}$ . In this work, emissions are first gridded at a resolution of  $1\\mathrm{km}\\times 1\\mathrm{km}$  and then regridded to a resolution of  $0.667^{\\circ}$  long  $\\times 0.5^{\\circ}$  lat to fit the GEOS-Chem model. The first two emission datasets evaluate two types of common spatial proxy maps: population distributions and nighttime lights. In the first gridded emission dataset (S1), all emissions are allocated based on population densities"}

            --- Evidence Item ---
            ID: chunk_003541
            Source Tool: Left_Path(Semantic)
            Metadata: {"paperid": "paper_002", "chunkid": "chunk_003541", "type": "texture", "metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Quantifying on-road vehicle emissions during traffic congestion using updated emission factors of light-duty gasoline vehicles and real-world traffic monitoring big data\", \"md_filename\": \"Quantifying on-road vehicle emissions during traff.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 25, \"content_length\": 1094, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "paper_id": "paper_002"}
            Content: {"text": "studies (Lu et al., 2021). Previous studies have reported the traffic congestion emissions from a limited number of vehicles and certain roads (i.e., one signalized intersection) (Alobaidi et al., 2020; Choudhary and Gokhale, 2019), but the real-world traffic congestion emissions for the typical urban road networks have rarely been reported. Ko et al. (2019) tested real-world driving emissions of a Euro-6 diesel vehicle and reported that  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions under the heavy traffic conditions were  $29\\%$  higher than those under smooth traffic conditions. Alobaidi et al. (2020) revealed a significant impact of the congestion on air pollution at intersections with  $8 - 25\\%$  reductions achieved by optimizing cycle and phase times, and preventing vehicles from stopping near the signalized intersection. The main obstacle towards accurately quantifying traffic congestion emissions is mainly associated with the lack of high temporal and spatial resolution observations of on-road traffic monitoring data and real-time online vehicle emission calculation platforms."}

            Metadata: {"metadata": "{\"paper_title\": \"Quantifying on-road vehicle emissions during traffic congestion using updated emission factors of light-duty gasoline vehicles and real-world traffic monitoring big data\", \"md_filename\": \"Quantifying on-road vehicle emissions during traff.md\", \"paragraph_index\": 122, \"content_length\": 1049, \"chunk_type\": \"text\"}", "chunkid": "chunk_003633", "type": "texture", "paperid": "paper_002", "paper_id": "paper_002"}
            Content: {"text": "standard upgrading, and even increased on-road vehicle emissions instead (see S1F2, S2F2, S3F2 in Table 2). Similarly, if the traffic congestion were effectively alleviated (S4F0) during the off-peak hours, the hourly emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  could be reduced by  $-14.2\\%$ ,  $-21.4\\%$ , and  $-9.6\\%$  respectively, but increased by  $7.8\\%$ ,  $11.4\\%$ , and  $5.2\\%$  if the congestions were aggravated (S6F0) (Figs. S11-S13, Table 3). Note that, in the southern areas of the district, the emission intensities in the arterial and residential streets far exceeded ( $\\sim 5$  times) those in the northern areas (Fig. 7, Fig. S11). These high emission hotspots were mainly attributed to the high traffic fluxes and low traffic speeds. Frequent traffic congestion, accelerations and decelerations led to significant increases in vehicles' fuel consumption. Thus, better traffic management policies should be applied to reduce vehicles' energy consumption and mitigate pollutant emissions as well (Huang et al., 2020b)."}

Similarly, if the traffic congestion were effectively alleviated (S4F0) during the off-peak hours, the hourly emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  could be reduced by  $-14.2\\%$ ,  $-21.4\\%$ , and  $-9.6\\%$  respectively, but increased by  $7.8\\%$ ,  $11.4\\%$ , and  $5.2\\%$  if the congestions were aggravated (S6F0) (Figs. S11-S13, Table 3). Note that, in the southern areas of the district, the emission intensities in the arterial and residential streets far exceeded ( $\\sim 5$  times) those in the northern areas (Fig. 7, Fig. S11). These high emission hotspots were mainly attributed to the high traffic fluxes and low traffic speeds. Frequent traffic congestion, accelerations and decelerations led to significant increases in vehicles' fuel consumption. Thus, better traffic management policies should be applied to reduce vehicles' energy consumption and mitigate pollutant emissions as well (Huang et al., 2020b)."}

        # Mission Overview
        You are the **Chief Scientist** in a scientific discovery team.
        Your team's goal is to investigate literature deeply and broadly, starting from the user's question, and discover new, valuable, and verifiable scientific knowledge.
        You are building a **research evidence tree** from retrieved and reviewed papers.

        # Team Roles
        You lead the direction of retrieval.
        Evaluators execute your retrieval plans, inspect text and figures, summarize findings, and assess whether each result should be expanded.
        Thinkers later synthesize validated evidence into a final scientific report.

        # Your Core Responsibilities
        1. **Initial planning**: for a new user question, propose multiple (3-5) search angles.
        2. **Dynamic decisions**: adapt based on evaluator feedback.
        3. **Direction control**: balance **breadth** (new directions) and **depth** (evidence-chain validation).

        # Decision Rules
        - When continuing retrieval, output a JSON list of strategy objects.
        - Each strategy object must have:
          - `action`: fixed as `call_tool`
          - `ParentNode`: evidence node ID (e.g., `chunk_xxx`) or `"0"` for global planning
          - `tool_name`: usually `strategy_semantic_search`, `strategy_metadata_search` or `strategy_exact_search`
          - `args`: tool arguments
          - `reason`: concise rationale

        ## Strategy Types
        1. **ParentNode Selection**:
           - Set to `"0"` for **BROADER (Exploration)**: discovering new directions when results are repetitive or sparse.
           - Set to a specific node ID (e.g., `"chunk_xxx"`) for **DEEPER (Exploitation)**: following up on a promising node to build an evidence chain.

        2. **Search Methods**:
           - `strategy_semantic_search`: Used for semantic similarity retrieval. Parameter: `query_intent` (a natural language phrase or sentence describing what you are looking for).
           - `strategy_metadata_search`: Used for retrieving a specific paper to explore its context. Parameter: `paper_id` (the ID of the paper).
           - `strategy_exact_search`: Used for exact text matching in the database. Parameter: `query_intent`. **IMPORTANT**: For exact search, you MUST use ONLY one or two specific proper nouns (e.g. "PM2.5" or "CNN-LSTM") rather than long phrases or sentences to prevent getting zero results. You should heavily refer to the `suggested_keywords` provided in the evaluation feedback.

        Before outputting each strategy, internally check:
        - Relevance to the original user question (0-10)
        - Novelty vs historical strategies (0-10)
        Only output if both are >= 7.

        # Historical Strategies
        Use historical strategies to avoid duplication and to improve novelty.

                        Node content: {'text': 'defines VOCs as any compound that participates in atmospheric photochemical reactions except for those that they designate as having minimal reactivity. The term oxygenated VOC (OVOC) (Goldstein and Galbally, 2007; Kwan et al., 2006) has further blurred these definitions, with colloquial usage sometimes being ambiguous as to whether OVOCs are a subset of VOCs or whether VOCs represent the un-oxygenated (i.e., non-methane hydrocarbon, NMHC) suite of compounds. Volatility-based nomenclature separates VOCs from semi-volatile OC (SVOC) and intermediate-volatility OC (IVOC) species (Robinson et al., 2007). For this study, we use NMOGs, which encompasses all gas-phase organic compounds (excluding methane), regardless of volatility, degree of oxygenation, or other chemical properties.'}. Paper ID: paper_025. Keywords: None

                Node content: {'text': 'The results of this work emphasize the importance of spatial proxies for bottom-up emission inventory development. Discrepancies between models and observations should be attributed to not only errors in the magnitude of total emission estimates but also spatial proxies. Although the selection of spatial proxies in this work is still empirical and may not represent the best case, we illustrate methods for improving gridded emission inventories by carefully selecting spatial proxies. This study provides a framework to apply information from satellite observations to inform bottom-up inventory development. The approach used here could be further extended to other species and regions, and more advanced optimized approaches could be introduced into the development of emission inventories of different air pollu'}. Ignoring errors in  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions introduces uncertainty in quantifying the impact of NMVOC emissions on surface  $\\mathrm{O}_3$ , especially in areas where overestimated  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions can unrealistically amplify titration effects, highlighting the necessity of simultaneous optimization of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions.'}. Evidence node ID: chunk_002902. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'OVOC photochemical aging visualization methods', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 5, 'credibility': 8}, action=[PRUNE].
                        Evaluator insight: OVOC concentrations range from pptv (clean areas) to ppbv (polluted areas) via direct emissions and NMHC oxidation pathways.. Reason: General background knowledge with no novel findings or unresolved questions.. Suggested keywords: ['concentration gradients', 'primary vs secondary VOCs'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'to a few hundred pptv (Galbally et al., 2007; Colomb et al., 2009; Wohl et al., 2020), while the concentrations of OVOCs in polluted areas can be as high as dozens of ppbv (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016; Sheng et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2019; Maji et al., 2020). The variation in OVOC concentrations is due to the wide variety and complexity of sources of OVOCs. OVOCs can be directly emitted by anthropogenic sources, e.g., solvent volatilization, vehicle emissions (Legreid et al., 2007; Ou et al., 2015), and biogenic sources (Jacob et al., 2002; Mellouki et al., 2015). In addition, OVOCs can also be formed as secondary products by photochemical oxidation of nonmethane hydrocarbons (NMHCs) from anthropogenic and biogenic sources (Yuan et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2015).'}. Paper ID: paper_014. Keywords: None

Evidence node ID: chunk_004262. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'visualizing PMF source uncertainty and photochemical interference', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 8, 'credibility': 8}, action=[GROW].
                        Evaluator insight: Vehicle emission profiles show greater PMF modeling discrepancy than other sources due to atmospheric photochemistry altering VOC ratios.. Reason: Identifies vehicle emissions as particularly vulnerable to misattribution in receptor models due to photochemical decay.. Suggested keywords: ['photochemical VOC decay', 'vehicle emission tracers', 'PMF collinearity'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'results by the PMF modelling. The measured and initial datasets were both introduced into the PMF model and the source profiles (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3) from two datasets were compared as shown in Fig. 8. The source profiles of gasoline evaporation, solvent usage, combustion sources, and industrial sources derived from two different datasets were in line with each other, with Pearson coefficient  $(r^2)$  of 0.99, 0.85, 0.68, and 0.40, respectively. However, a great discrepancy was found for vehicle emissions. The PMF algorithms aim to find the profiles and contributions for a given number of factors that have the minimum weighed residues of the input dataset. If the importance of atmospheric photochemical processes exceeds the differences of emission profiles from various sources, the factors in PMF can still be identified according to the different degrees of photochemical processing (Yuan et al., 2012). The source profiles derived from the two datasets suggested that the importance of photochemistry in the atmosphere exceeded the differences in the VOC compositions of vehicle emissions. Compared to the source apportionment from the measured dataset, the contributions of solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources to total VOC mixing ratio derived from the initial dataset were higher, while vehicle emissions and combustion sources were lower (Fig. S4). The differences suggested that the VOC decay should be considered before source apportionment using the receptor model.'}. Paper ID: paper_006. Keywords: None

Evidence node ID: chunk_002802. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'multi-proxy emission mapping validation techniques', 'n_results': 20}.
                This is hit #1 (duplicate hit).
                Status: ⏳ [PENDING/DUPLICATE] (no fresh detailed evaluation in this cycle; refer to historical info/metadata).
                Node content: {'text': 'Abstract. Spatial proxies used in bottom-up emission inventories to derive the spatial distributions of emissions are usually empirical and involve additional levels of uncertainty. Although uncertainties in current emission inventories have been discussed extensively, uncertainties resulting from improper spatial proxies have rarely been evaluated. In this work, we investigate the impact of spatial proxies on the representation of gridded emissions by comparing six gridded  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission datasets over China developed from the same magnitude of emissions and different spatial proxies. GEOS-Chem-modeled tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns simulated from different gridded emission inventories are compared with satellite-based columns. The results show that differences between modeled and satellite-based  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns are sensitive to the spatial proxies used in the gridded emission inventories. The total population density is less suitable for allocating  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions than nighttime light data because population density tends to allocate more emissions to rural areas. Determining the exact locations of large emission sources could significantly strengthen the correlation between modeled and observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns. Using vehicle population and an updated road network for the on-road transport sector could substantially en'}. Paper ID: paper_022. Keywords: None

                        Node content: {'text': "standard upgrading, and even increased on-road vehicle emissions instead (see S1F2, S2F2, S3F2 in Table 2). Similarly, if the traffic congestion were effectively alleviated (S4F0) during the off-peak hours, the hourly emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  could be reduced by  $-14.2\\%$ ,  $-21.4\\%$ , and  $-9.6\\%$  respectively, but increased by  $7.8\\%$ ,  $11.4\\%$ , and  $5.2\\%$  if the congestions were aggravated (S6F0) (Figs. S11-S13, Table 3). Note that, in the southern areas of the district, the emission intensities in the arterial and residential streets far exceeded ( $\\sim 5$  times) those in the northern areas (Fig. 7, Fig. S11). These high emission hotspots were mainly attributed to the high traffic fluxes and low traffic speeds. Thus, better traffic management policies should be applied to reduce vehicles' energy consumption and mitigate pollutant emissions as well (Huang et al., 2020b)."}. Paper ID: paper_002. Evidence node ID: chunk_002806. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'spatial mapping of vehicle emission hotspots', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #2. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 9, 'credibility': 8}, action=[GROW].
                        Evaluator insight: Industrial emissions frequently rely on spatial proxies due to data limitations, introducing potential biases despite more accurate direct-coordinate methods being available for point sources.. Reason: Core taxonomy of proxy types with sector-specific applications and multiple source citations. Suggested keywords: ['spatial proxy taxonomy', 'industrial emission allocation', 'mobile-source uncertainty'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'Many methods for allocating regional emission totals to grids are available. The most accurate approach involves allocating emissions for which the actual latitude-longitude coordinates of the emitting facilities (e.g., power plants or cement plants) are available. For mobile and area sources (i.e., sources for which the exact emission locations are unknown), parameters of so-called spatial proxies must be used to represent the spatial distributions of emissions. For example, emissions from road transportation sources can be allocated based on road networks, and residential emissions that are strongly related to human activities can be gridded using population densities or nighttime lights (Streets et al., 2003; Woo et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Zhang et al., 2009; Oda et al., 2011). In many cases, industrial emissions are also allocated by proxies because of the limited information available.'}. Paper ID: paper_022. Keywords: None

                        Node content: {'text': 'obtained from the LandScan population database (ORNL, 2006). The Gridded Population of the World (GPW) population map is also frequently used to allocate emissions, but we do not include it in our analysis because the uncertainties introduced by differences in population maps are minor (Andres et al., 2016). In the second dataset (S2), all emissions are allocated based on the nighttime lights map drawn from the Defense Meteorological Satellite Program Operational Linescan System (DMSP-OSL) satellite (http://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/dmsp/download_rad_cal_96-97.html). These two scenarios, which involve a single type of spatial proxy for all sectors, are simplifications of the common practice utilized in current emission inventories. We use S1 as the base case and make modifications upon it to evaluate different spatial proxies used in different sectors. Figure 1 compares the spatial distributions of the total population and nighttime light data. The nighttime light data present more significant urban-rural gradients than the total population density data and, thus, may better represent differences in economic development levels between urban and rural areas.'}. Evidence node ID: chunk_002888. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'multi-proxy emission mapping validation techniques', 'n_results': 20}.
                This is hit #1 (duplicate hit).
                Status: ⏳ [PENDING/DUPLICATE] (no fresh detailed evaluation in this cycle; refer to historical info/metadata).
                Node content: {'text': 'The spatial proxies used when developing gridded emission inventories are empirical and can introduce uncertainties in bottom-up emission inventories. This issue has rarely been evaluated. In this work, we evaluate the effects of spatial proxies on the representations of spatial distributions of emissions using an integrated framework of bottom-up emission inventories, a chemical transport model, and satellite observations. We first develop six sets of gridded  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions for China using the same magnitude of emissions from the MEIC and different spatial proxies. The spatial proxies considered in this study include the following: total population, nighttime lights, locations of power plants, IGDP, vehicle populations, and two different road network datasets. The nested-grid GEOS-Chem model is then used to simulate tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns using the six gridded emissions, and modeled  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns are compared to satellite-based  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns derived from OMI data.'}. Paper ID: paper_022. Keywords: None

--------------------------------------------------
Evidence node ID: chunk_002945. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'OVOC photochemical aging visualization methods', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 10, 'credibility': 9}, action=[GROW].
                        Evaluator insight: OVOC source quantification reveals compound-specific patterns: methanol/MEK predominately anthropogenic (47-51%), acetone highly background-influenced (37%), and formic acid dominated by secondary anthropogenic sources (34%).. Reason: Quantitative source apportionment with compound-specific insights provides foundational data for chemical mechanism validation.. Suggested keywords: ['compound-specific apportionment', 'secondary anthropogenic sources', 'background mixing ratios'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\\%)$'}. Paper ID: paper_014. Keywords: None

                        Evaluator insight: High-emitting vehicles (top 10% of fleet) disproportionately contribute to total emissions, but their travel behaviors and activity trajectories remain uncharacterized.. Reason: Directly addresses knowledge gaps regarding high emitters' spatial-temporal patterns with concrete methodological proposals for follow-up studies.. Suggested keywords: ['high emitter trajectories', 'vehicle-specific activity matching', 'congestion-aware emission models'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'Due to the limited time span of the vehicle emissions dataset, the interannual variation characteristics of emissions of the high emitters cannot be further analyzed. Future research can fill this gap to explore more suitable cleaning alternative cycles for the high emitters. This study has investigated the contributions of the  $10\\%$  high emitters to the total fleet emissions, but the travel behavior and typical activity trajectories of these high emitters still need to be explored. In the follow-up research, the driving trajectories and hot spots of high emission vehicles can be obtained based on the accurate matching of vehicle-specific emission factors and vehicle-specific activity data, so as to provide more effective support for the managements and controls of target high emission vehicles. The analysis of environmental impacts of traffic congestion reveals the importance of traffic congestion controls on air pollutant reductions. Existing on-road vehicle emission models (i.e., IVE and COPERT (Choudhary and Gokhale, 2019)) usually lack considerations of traffic congestion when calculating on-road emissions, so it can be optimized in future research to better reflect the realistic on-road emissions.'}. Paper ID: paper_002. Keywords: None

                        Evaluator insight: Photochemical OVOC source analysis assumes: 1) proportional tracer-VOC emissions, 2) OH radical scavenging dominance, 3) isoprene-based biogenic scaling, with major uncertainties in tracer validity and emission ratios.. Reason: Details core assumptions and uncertainty drivers of key methodology; critical for evaluating reliability of Chinese OVOC apportionments.. Suggested keywords: ['anthropogenic tracer ratio', 'OH reactivity', 'biogenic emission scaling'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'When using photochemical-age-based parametric methods to analyze the sources of OVOCs, de Gouw et al. (2005) proposed the following assumptions: (1) anthropogenic emissions of OVOCs and their precursors are in direct proportion to the selected tracers of primary emissions; (2) OVOCs are mainly scavenged by reactions with OH radicals during the daytime; and (3) the biogenic source of OVOCs is proportional to the emission of isoprene. Therefore, the uncertainty of the method mainly depends on the effectiveness of the selected tracer, the assumed stable emission ratio and the simplified variability of VOC properties (de Gouw et al., 2005). In this study, some modifications were made to the equation based on the above assumptions to make the model more suitable for the'}. Paper ID: paper_014. Keywords: None

                        Node content: {'text': 'studies (Lu et al., 2021). Previous studies have reported the traffic congestion emissions from a limited number of vehicles and certain roads (i.e., one signalized intersection) (Alobaidi et al., 2020; Choudhary and Gokhale, 2019), but the real-world traffic congestion emissions for the typical urban road networks have rarely been reported. Ko et al. (2019) tested real-world driving emissions of a Euro-6 diesel vehicle and reported that  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions under the heavy traffic conditions were  $29\\%$  higher than those under smooth traffic conditions. Alobaidi et al. (2020) revealed a significant impact of the congestion on air pollution at intersections with  $8 - 25\\%$  reductions achieved by optimizing cycle and phase times, and preventing vehicles from stopping near the signalized intersection. The main obstacle towards accurately quantifying traffic congestion emissions is mainly associated with the lack of high temporal and spatial resolution observations of on-road traffic monitoring data and real-time online vehicle emission calculation platforms.'}. Paper ID: paper_002. Keywords: None

Evidence node ID: chunk_002971. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'OVOC photochemical aging visualization methods', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 9, 'credibility': 8}, action=[GROW].
                        Evaluator insight: OVOCs dominate Shenzhen's VOC composition (70-78%) with distinct seasonal (dry: 33.1 ppb vs wet: 22.9 ppb) and diurnal patterns; acetaldehyde showed strongest O3 correlation and highest ozone formation potential; primary anthropogenic OVOCs decreased 50-60% (2014-2019) from control measures while secondary anthropogenic sources require further attention. Reason: Concrete observational data with quantified trends, explicit correlation to O3 formation, policy impacts, and clear research gaps for secondary source controls. Suggested keywords: ['OVOC diurnal variation', 'acetaldehyde OFP', 'photochemical aging parameterization', 'anthropogenic secondary sources'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'PTR-MS was used for continuous online monitoring of atmospheric VOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019. The results revealed that OVOCs were the most abundant of 17 species of VOCs measured, comprising  $70 - 78\\%$ . The six-year mean concentration for the dry season was  $33.1~\\mathrm{ppb}$ , approximately  $13\\%$  higher than that for the wet season of  $22.9~\\mathrm{ppb}$ . There were diurnal variations in OVOC concentrations, with higher concentrations during the daytime. The diurnal variation in acetaldehyde was most similar to that of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$ , and it had the largest OFP of the OVOC species, indicating that acetaldehyde was an important intermediate of photochemical reactions. The results from source apportionment based on photochemical aging parameterization revealed that contributions of OVOCs from primary anthropogenic sources exhibited an evident declining trend of  $50 - 60\\%$  from 2014 to 2019 during the dry season, indicating that the intensive VOC control measures implemented in the northern inland areas of the sampling site had a significant effect on the reduction of primary emissions. However, to reduce the impact of OVOCs on air quality in Shenzhen, more efforts are needed to control OVOCs from anthropogenic secondary sources by reducing not only their primary VOC precursors but also their complex atmospheric oxidizing capacity, which will finally restrict the production of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  and SOA.'}. Paper ID: paper_014. Keywords: None

                        Node content: {'text': "The figure compares predicted and measured emission factors of PM's chemical constituents from different studies, showcasing performance variations against the STEAM2 model. Evidence node ID: chunk_002903. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'OVOC photochemical aging visualization methods', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 8, 'credibility': 9}, action=[KEEP].
                        Evaluator insight: OVOCs drive ozone/SOA formation and health risks in China, with carbonyls showing 2.5× higher O3 formation than BTEX in Beijing, and >70% urban contributions from industrial/vehicular sources.. Reason: Synthesizes multi-city evidence on OVOC impacts but limited expansion potential due to consolidated findings format.. Suggested keywords: ['carbonyl O3 potential', 'PTR-MS measurement', 'anthropogenic source dominance'].
                        Node content: {'text': "Previous studies have proven that some low-carbon OVOCs (formaldehyde and acetaldehyde) can generate OH radicals and hydroperoxyl radicals  $\\mathrm{(HO_2)}$  through photolysis to affect the oxidizing capacity of the atmosphere (Volkamer et al., 2010; Shao et al., 2011; Xue et al., 2016). OVOCs can also participate in photochemical reactions and contribute to the generation of  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  and SOA (Louie et al., 2013; Ehn et al., 2014). In addition, as most OVOCs are highly water soluble, they can be absorbed into the human body, irritating the eyes, skin and respiratory tract, thus harming human health. OVOCs such as formaldehyde, acetaldehyde, acetone and acrolein have been shown to pose serious health risks (WHO, 2000, Delfino et al., 2003; Bashkin, 2009; Du et al., 2014). Given the important role of OVOCs in photochemical reactions and the complexity of their sources, OVOCs have been the subject of extensive research recently (Sahu et al., 2017; Maji et al., 2020), especially in China, where air pollution is severe (Zhang et al., 2012, 2015; Zhou et al., 2019; Zhu et al., 2019). Zhang et al. (2012) studied the pollution characteristics of BTEX (benzene, toluene, ethylbenzene, m,p-xylene and o-xylene) and carbonyl compounds during summer in Beijing and found that the  $\\mathrm{O_3}$  formation potential of carbonyl compounds  $(166.1~\\mu \\mathrm{g} / \\mathrm{m}^3)$  was approximately 2.5 times that of BTEX  $(65.4~\\mu \\mathrm{g} / \\mathrm{m}^3)$ . Zhou et al. (2019) used proton transfer reaction mass spectrometry (PTR-MS) to measure VOCs in Xi'an and used the positive matrix factorization (PMF) model to analyze over 30 types of VOCs and identify eight primary sources and found that local emissions, such as industrial processes, solvent usage, residential biofuel use and vehicular exhaust, contributed about  $70\\%$  of the VOCs measured. Zhu et al. (2019) used PTR-MS to monitor OVOCs during different seasons and applied photochemical age parameter analysis to evaluate the sources of OVOCs in Shenzhen. They found that primary anthropogenic sources were the most important source of OVOCs in urban areas of China."}. Paper ID: paper_014. Keywords: None

Evidence node ID: chunk_003220. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'visualizing PMF source uncertainty and photochemical interference', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 8, 'credibility': 9}, action=[GROW].
                        Evaluator insight: Tunnel measurements confirmed that non-combustion sources (tire/brake wear, resuspended dust) contribute significantly to PM2.5, with PM2.5/PM10 ratios of ~0.5 indicating coarse-particle dominance from HDDV interactions.. Reason: Empirically distinguishes combustion vs. non-combustion PM sources and quantifies HDDV impact under controlled conditions. Highly actionable for urban PM management.. Suggested keywords: ['non-exhaust emissions', 'tire-road interaction', 'HDDV contribution', 'primary organic aerosols'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'The average  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  and  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  concentrations were in the ranges of 41.0-87.5 and  $23.5 - 41.3\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3}$ , respectively. The PM concentrations varied substantially compared with the other pollutants (Fig. 2g and h). The maximum  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  concentration  $(188\\mu \\mathrm{g}\\mathrm{m}^{-3})$  was observed when the mobile laboratory passed next to a HDDV, indicating that PM can be strongly affected by the number of HDDVs on the road, which accounted for a relatively high proportion  $(9\\%)$  of the traffic volume in the SJT. Non-combustion PM sources in the road environment include tire wear, brake abrasion, and resuspended dust (Abu-Allaban et al., 2003). Because photochemical reactions are rare in tunnels, the main contributors of  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5}$  were primary sources, i.e., elemental carbon and primary organic aerosols from vehicle emissions and small particles from non-combustion emissions. The  $\\mathrm{PM}_{2.5} / \\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  ratio remained relatively constant during the campaign (approximately 0.5), indicative of the mixed effects of vehicle combustion and non-combustion sources. Therefore, the intermittent increases in the  $\\mathrm{PM}_{10}$  concentration due to HDDVs was likely caused by tire-road interactions (such as the resuspension of road dust, tire wear, and other factors). The concentration gradients and detailed statistical information are presented in Table S3.'}. Paper ID: paper_027. Keywords: None

Evidence node ID: chunk_002885. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'multi-proxy emission mapping validation techniques', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 9, 'credibility': 8}, action=[GROW].
                        Evaluator insight: Spatial proxies introduce empirical uncertainties in bottom-up inventories, with satellite validation revealing >⁠0.25° errors in urban-scale emissions accuracy.. Reason: High-value evidence quantifying inventory spatial uncertainty using novel satellite-ground integration approach.. Suggested keywords: ['spatial proxy uncertainty', 'satellite-inventory fusion', 'urban-scale validation'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'As shown in Sect. 2.1 and described in this work, regardless of how spatial proxies are adjusted in a bottom-up inventory, they are always empirical and contribute uncertainties to the spatial representation of emission inventories. A companion paper of this work found that large uncertainties existed in proxy-based inventories on the urban scale (less than  $0.25^{\\circ}$ ) by comparing the proxy-based inventory with an inventory developed from exact locations of emitting facilities in Hebei, China (Zheng et al., 2017). Critical evaluations must be conducted to ensure the accuracy of these proxies. Our work presents a practical means to diagnose this problem and involves using satellite observations as an indicator of ground emissions to determine the relationships between emissions and local parameters. Our approach also has the attribute of propagating information from satellite observations into bottom-up inventory developments.'}. Paper ID: paper_022. Keywords: None

Reason: Quantifies congestion-emission relationships with spatial hotspots and policy implications.. Suggested keywords: ['congestion mitigation', 'emission hotspots', 'traffic flux intensity', 'acceleration-deceleration emissions'].
                        Node content: {'text': "standard upgrading, and even increased on-road vehicle emissions instead (see S1F2, S2F2, S3F2 in Table 2). Similarly, if the traffic congestion were effectively alleviated (S4F0) during the off-peak hours, the hourly emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  could be reduced by  $-14.2\\%$ ,  $-21.4\\%$ , and  $-9.6\\%$  respectively, but increased by  $7.8\\%$ ,  $11.4\\%$ , and  $5.2\\%$  if the congestions were aggravated (S6F0) (Figs. S11-S13, Table 3). Note that, in the southern areas of the district, the emission intensities in the arterial and residential streets far exceeded ( $\\sim 5$  times) those in the northern areas (Fig. 7, Fig. S11). These high emission hotspots were mainly attributed to the high traffic fluxes and low traffic speeds. Frequent traffic congestion, accelerations and decelerations led to significant increases in vehicles' fuel consumption. Thus, better traffic management policies should be applied to reduce vehicles' energy consumption and mitigate pollutant emissions as well (Huang et al., 2020b)."}. Paper ID: paper_002.                         Node content: {'text': 'Due to the limited time span of the vehicle emissions dataset, the interannual variation characteristics of emissions of the high emitters cannot be further analyzed. Future research can fill this gap to explore more suitable cleaning alternative cycles for the high emitters. This study has investigated the contributions of the  $10\\%$  high emitters to the total fleet emissions, but the travel behavior and typical activity trajectories of these high emitters still need to be explored. In the follow-up research, the driving trajectories and hot spots of high emission vehicles can be obtained based on the accurate matching of vehicle-specific emission factors and vehicle-specific activity data, so as to provide more effective support for the managements and controls of target high emission vehicles. The analysis of environmental impacts of traffic congestion reveals the importance of traffic congestion controls on air pollutant reductions. Existing on-road vehicle emission models (i.e., IVE and COPERT (Choudhary and Gokhale, 2019)) usually lack considerations of traffic congestion when calculating on-road emissions, so it can be optimized in future research to better reflect the realistic on-road emissions.'}. Paper ID: paper_002. Keywords: None

                        Evaluator insight: Heavy traffic conditions increase NOx emissions by 29% compared to smooth traffic conditions, but quantifying real-world urban network congestion emissions is limited by insufficient high-resolution traffic data..                         Node content: {'text': 'studies (Lu et al., 2021). Previous studies have reported the traffic congestion emissions from a limited number of vehicles and certain roads (i.e., one signalized intersection) (Alobaidi et al., 2020; Choudhary and Gokhale, 2019), but the real-world traffic congestion emissions for the typical urban road networks have rarely been reported. Ko et al. (2019) tested real-world driving emissions of a Euro-6 diesel vehicle and reported that  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions under the heavy traffic conditions were  $29\\%$  higher than those under smooth traffic conditions. Alobaidi et al. (2020) revealed a significant impact of the congestion on air pollution at intersections with  $8 - 25\\%$  reductions achieved by optimizing cycle and phase times, and preventing vehicles from stopping near the signalized intersection. The main obstacle towards accurately quantifying traffic congestion emissions is mainly associated with the lack of high temporal and spatial resolution observations of on-road traffic monitoring data and real-time online vehicle emission calculation platforms.'}. Paper ID: paper_002. Keywords: None

                        Node content: {'text': 'We then develop six sets of gridded  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission data using the same magnitude of emissions from the MEIC and different spatial proxies, as presented in Table 3. Many geographic information system (GIS) grid-based spatial proxies (e.g., population density and road networks) are at a resolution of  $1\\mathrm{km}\\times 1\\mathrm{km}$ . In this work, emissions are first gridded at a resolution of  $1\\mathrm{km}\\times 1\\mathrm{km}$  and then regridded to a resolution of  $0.667^{\\circ}$  long  $\\times 0.5^{\\circ}$  lat to fit the GEOS-Chem model. The first two emission datasets evaluate two types of common spatial proxy maps: population distributions and nighttime lights. In the first gridded emission dataset (S1), all emissions are allocated based on population densities'}. Paper ID: paper_022. Keywords: None

                        This is hit #2. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 8, 'credibility': 9}, action=[GROW].
                        Evaluator insight: The mosaic method in HTAP_v2.2 increases spatial accuracy but causes source-location inconsistencies for multi-pollutant modeling and creates border artefacts.. Reason: High-value comparison between mosaic and unified approaches highlighting trade-offs. Suggested keywords: ['mosaic uncertainty', 'HTAP_v2.2 border artefacts', 'multi-pollutant consistency'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'This paper provides a detailed description of the data sets and of the methodology used to compute the  $0.1^{\\circ} \\times 0.1^{\\circ}$  grid maps for 2008 and 2010, which are delivered via the EDGAR JRC website (see Sect. 4). Section 2 defines the considered emitting sectors and presents the original data sources: (a) the officially accepted regional/national gridded emission inventories, which were mainly provided by national and international institutions, and (b) EDGAR_v4.3 for gap filling the remaining regions and/or sectors for some substances. In the HTAP_v2.2 database, grid maps were merged together with a "collage/mosaic" approach instead of gridding the global emission inventory with one single proxy data set, as done in for the EDGAR-HTAP_v1 data set compilation (JanssensMaenhout et al., 2012). The HTAP_v2.2 inventory aims to obtain more local accuracy on the location of single point sources compared to the previous HTAP_v1, but the downside is that a consistent single location of a specific source of multi-pollutants is no longer ensured, when data originated from different sources, possibly leading to spurious chemical reactions involving non-linear chemistry in the air quality models. Sect. 3 discusses the resulting grid maps and addresses the contents of the HTAP_v2.2 compilation methodology, the assumptions, dataflows and consistency of the data used to create the global grid maps. Whereas HTAP_v2.2 uses more regional bottom-up data (local information on emission factors, on assumed penetration of technology and end-of-pipe control measures in the facilities), the higher spatial accuracy is sometimes overshadowed by artefacts at borders – at least when graphically displaying the data. This is followed with an evaluation of the HTAP_v2.2 by comparing per capita emissions, emissions per unit of GDP (gross domestic product) and implied emission factors for the different countries. The concluding Sect. 4 summarises the purposes, content and access to this data set that is currently in use by the HTAP modellers community.'}. Paper ID: paper_011. Keywords: None

Evidence node ID: chunk_004255. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'visualizing PMF source uncertainty and photochemical interference', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 3, 'credibility': 8}, action=[PRUNE].
                        Evaluator insight: PMF modeling uncertainty arises from chemical transformations of VOCs in the atmosphere, complicating source separation.. Reason: Discusses general VOC source-apportionment challenges without connections to traffic-specific emissions.. Suggested keywords: ['PMF model limitations', 'VOC photochemistry'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'For the PMF model derived uncertainty, it comes from four assumptions: (1) the source profiles do not change significantly over time; (2) the species do not react chemically or undergo physical processes; (3) the collected data are consistent with the conceptual model and represent the studied geographical area; and (4) equivalent/comparable analytical methods are applied to the receptor site throughout the study (Belis et al., 2013). However, the photochemistry of VOCs in the ambient air made it difficult to separate the sources derived from the PMF model and attention should be paid to the interpretation of the sources derived from the PMF model (Yuan et al., 2012).'}. Paper ID: paper_006. Keywords: None

                        Evaluator insight: NMOG terminology encompasses all non-methane organic gases regardless of volatility/oxygenation, resolving ambiguities among VOC/OVOC/IVOC classifications. Reason: Provides critical conceptual clarification for VOC categories but doesn't contribute new atmospheric findings. Suggested keywords: ['NMOG definition', 'volatility-based classification'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'defines VOCs as any compound that participates in atmospheric photochemical reactions except for those that they designate as having minimal reactivity. The term oxygenated VOC (OVOC) (Goldstein and Galbally, 2007; Kwan et al., 2006) has further blurred these definitions, with colloquial usage sometimes being ambiguous as to whether OVOCs are a subset of VOCs or whether VOCs represent the un-oxygenated (i.e., non-methane hydrocarbon, NMHC) suite of compounds. Volatility-based nomenclature separates VOCs from semi-volatile OC (SVOC) and intermediate-volatility OC (IVOC) species (Robinson et al., 2007). For this study, we use NMOGs, which encompasses all gas-phase organic compounds (excluding methane), regardless of volatility, degree of oxygenation, or other chemical properties.'}. Paper ID: paper_025. Keywords: None

                Status: ⏳ [PENDING/DUPLICATE] (no fresh detailed evaluation in this cycle; refer to historical info/metadata).
                Node content: {'text': 'The results of this work emphasize the importance of spatial proxies for bottom-up emission inventory development. Discrepancies between models and observations should be attributed to not only errors in the magnitude of total emission estimates but also spatial proxies. Although the selection of spatial proxies in this work is still empirical and may not represent the best case, we illustrate methods for improving gridded emission inventories by carefully selecting spatial proxies. This study provides a framework to apply information from satellite observations to inform bottom-up inventory development. The approach used here could be further extended to other species and regions, and more advanced optimized approaches could be introduced into the development of emission inventories of different air pollu'}.                 Node content: {'text': 'Sensitivity inversions demonstrate the robustness of top-down emissions to variations in prior uncertainty settings, yet they are sensitive to HCHO column biases, highlighting the importance of comprehensive validation studies utilizing available remote-sensing data and, if possible, airborne validation campaigns. Moreover, we found that, in comparison to optimizing NMVOC emissions alone, the joint optimization of NMVOC and  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions can significantly improve the overall performance of  $\\mathrm{O}_3$  simulations. Ignoring errors in  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions introduces uncertainty in quantifying the impact of NMVOC emissions on surface  $\\mathrm{O}_3$ , especially in areas where overestimated  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions can unrealistically amplify titration effects, highlighting the necessity of simultaneous optimization of  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions.'}. Evidence node ID: chunk_002902. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'OVOC photochemical aging visualization methods', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 5, 'credibility': 8}, action=[PRUNE].
                        Evaluator insight: OVOC concentrations range from pptv (clean areas) to ppbv (polluted areas) via direct emissions and NMHC oxidation pathways.. Reason: General background knowledge with no novel findings or unresolved questions.. Suggested keywords: ['concentration gradients', 'primary vs secondary VOCs'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'to a few hundred pptv (Galbally et al., 2007; Colomb et al., 2009; Wohl et al., 2020), while the concentrations of OVOCs in polluted areas can be as high as dozens of ppbv (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016; Sheng et al., 2018; Zhou et al., 2019; Maji et al., 2020). The variation in OVOC concentrations is due to the wide variety and complexity of sources of OVOCs. OVOCs can be directly emitted by anthropogenic sources, e.g., solvent volatilization, vehicle emissions (Legreid et al., 2007; Ou et al., 2015), and biogenic sources (Jacob et al., 2002; Mellouki et al., 2015). In addition, OVOCs can also be formed as secondary products by photochemical oxidation of nonmethane hydrocarbons (NMHCs) from anthropogenic and biogenic sources (Yuan et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2015).'}. Paper ID: paper_014. Keywords: None

Evidence node ID: chunk_004262. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'visualizing PMF source uncertainty and photochemical interference', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 8, 'credibility': 8}, action=[GROW].
                        Evaluator insight: Vehicle emission profiles show greater PMF modeling discrepancy than other sources due to atmospheric photochemistry altering VOC ratios.. Reason: Identifies vehicle emissions as particularly vulnerable to misattribution in receptor models due to photochemical decay.. Suggested keywords: ['photochemical VOC decay', 'vehicle emission tracers', 'PMF collinearity'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'results by the PMF modelling. The measured and initial datasets were both introduced into the PMF model and the source profiles (Fig. 4 and Fig. S3) from two datasets were compared as shown in Fig. 8. The source profiles of gasoline evaporation, solvent usage, combustion sources, and industrial sources derived from two different datasets were in line with each other, with Pearson coefficient  $(r^2)$  of 0.99, 0.85, 0.68, and 0.40, respectively. However, a great discrepancy was found for vehicle emissions. The PMF algorithms aim to find the profiles and contributions for a given number of factors that have the minimum weighed residues of the input dataset. If the importance of atmospheric photochemical processes exceeds the differences of emission profiles from various sources, the factors in PMF can still be identified according to the different degrees of photochemical processing (Yuan et al., 2012). The source profiles derived from the two datasets suggested that the importance of photochemistry in the atmosphere exceeded the differences in the VOC compositions of vehicle emissions. Compared to the source apportionment from the measured dataset, the contributions of solvent usage, gasoline evaporation, and industrial sources to total VOC mixing ratio derived from the initial dataset were higher, while vehicle emissions and combustion sources were lower (Fig. S4). The differences suggested that the VOC decay should be considered before source apportionment using the receptor model.'}. Paper ID: paper_006. Keywords: None

Evidence node ID: chunk_002802. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'multi-proxy emission mapping validation techniques', 'n_results': 20}.
                This is hit #1 (duplicate hit).
                Status: ⏳ [PENDING/DUPLICATE] (no fresh detailed evaluation in this cycle; refer to historical info/metadata).
                Node content: {'text': 'Abstract. Spatial proxies used in bottom-up emission inventories to derive the spatial distributions of emissions are usually empirical and involve additional levels of uncertainty. Although uncertainties in current emission inventories have been discussed extensively, uncertainties resulting from improper spatial proxies have rarely been evaluated. In this work, we investigate the impact of spatial proxies on the representation of gridded emissions by comparing six gridded  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emission datasets over China developed from the same magnitude of emissions and different spatial proxies. GEOS-Chem-modeled tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns simulated from different gridded emission inventories are compared with satellite-based columns. The results show that differences between modeled and satellite-based  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns are sensitive to the spatial proxies used in the gridded emission inventories. The total population density is less suitable for allocating  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions than nighttime light data because population density tends to allocate more emissions to rural areas. Determining the exact locations of large emission sources could significantly strengthen the correlation between modeled and observed  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns. Using vehicle population and an updated road network for the on-road transport sector could substantially en'}. Paper ID: paper_022. Keywords: None

                        Node content: {'text': "standard upgrading, and even increased on-road vehicle emissions instead (see S1F2, S2F2, S3F2 in Table 2). Similarly, if the traffic congestion were effectively alleviated (S4F0) during the off-peak hours, the hourly emissions of CO, HC, and  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  could be reduced by  $-14.2\\%$ ,  $-21.4\\%$ , and  $-9.6\\%$  respectively, but increased by  $7.8\\%$ ,  $11.4\\%$ , and  $5.2\\%$  if the congestions were aggravated (S6F0) (Figs. S11-S13, Table 3). Note that, in the southern areas of the district, the emission intensities in the arterial and residential streets far exceeded ( $\\sim 5$  times) those in the northern areas (Fig. 7, Fig. S11). These high emission hotspots were mainly attributed to the high traffic fluxes and low traffic speeds. Frequent traffic congestion, accelerations and decelerations led to significant increases in vehicles' fuel consumption. Thus, better traffic management policies should be applied to reduce vehicles' energy consumption and mitigate pollutant emissions as well (Huang et al., 2020b)."}. Paper ID: paper_002. Evidence node ID: chunk_002806. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'spatial mapping of vehicle emission hotspots', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #2. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 9, 'credibility': 8}, action=[GROW].
                        Evaluator insight: Industrial emissions frequently rely on spatial proxies due to data limitations, introducing potential biases despite more accurate direct-coordinate methods being available for point sources.. Reason: Core taxonomy of proxy types with sector-specific applications and multiple source citations. Suggested keywords: ['spatial proxy taxonomy', 'industrial emission allocation', 'mobile-source uncertainty'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'Many methods for allocating regional emission totals to grids are available. The most accurate approach involves allocating emissions for which the actual latitude-longitude coordinates of the emitting facilities (e.g., power plants or cement plants) are available. For mobile and area sources (i.e., sources for which the exact emission locations are unknown), parameters of so-called spatial proxies must be used to represent the spatial distributions of emissions. For example, emissions from road transportation sources can be allocated based on road networks, and residential emissions that are strongly related to human activities can be gridded using population densities or nighttime lights (Streets et al., 2003; Woo et al., 2003; Ohara et al., 2007; Zhang et al., 2009; Oda et al., 2011). In many cases, industrial emissions are also allocated by proxies because of the limited information available.'}. Paper ID: paper_022. Keywords: None

                        Node content: {'text': 'obtained from the LandScan population database (ORNL, 2006). The Gridded Population of the World (GPW) population map is also frequently used to allocate emissions, but we do not include it in our analysis because the uncertainties introduced by differences in population maps are minor (Andres et al., 2016). In the second dataset (S2), all emissions are allocated based on the nighttime lights map drawn from the Defense Meteorological Satellite Program Operational Linescan System (DMSP-OSL) satellite (http://www.ngdc.noaa.gov/dmsp/download_rad_cal_96-97.html). These two scenarios, which involve a single type of spatial proxy for all sectors, are simplifications of the common practice utilized in current emission inventories. We use S1 as the base case and make modifications upon it to evaluate different spatial proxies used in different sectors. Figure 1 compares the spatial distributions of the total population and nighttime light data. The nighttime light data present more significant urban-rural gradients than the total population density data and, thus, may better represent differences in economic development levels between urban and rural areas.'}.                 Node content: {'text': 'The spatial proxies used when developing gridded emission inventories are empirical and can introduce uncertainties in bottom-up emission inventories. This issue has rarely been evaluated. In this work, we evaluate the effects of spatial proxies on the representations of spatial distributions of emissions using an integrated framework of bottom-up emission inventories, a chemical transport model, and satellite observations. We first develop six sets of gridded  $\\mathrm{NO}_x$  emissions for China using the same magnitude of emissions from the MEIC and different spatial proxies. The spatial proxies considered in this study include the following: total population, nighttime lights, locations of power plants, IGDP, vehicle populations, and two different road network datasets. The nested-grid GEOS-Chem model is then used to simulate tropospheric  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  vertical columns using the six gridded emissions, and modeled  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns are compared to satellite-based  $\\mathrm{NO}_2$  columns derived from OMI data.'}. Paper ID: paper_022. Keywords: None

Evidence node ID: chunk_002945. Retrieved via tool(s): strategy_semantic_search. Args: {'query_intent': 'OVOC photochemical aging visualization methods', 'n_results': 20}.
                        This is hit #1. Evaluator result: score={'relevance': 10, 'credibility': 9}, action=[GROW].
                        Evaluator insight: OVOC source quantification reveals compound-specific patterns: methanol/MEK predominately anthropogenic (47-51%), acetone highly background-influenced (37%), and formic acid dominated by secondary anthropogenic sources (34%).. Reason: Quantitative source apportionment with compound-specific insights provides foundational data for chemical mechanism validation.. Suggested keywords: ['compound-specific apportionment', 'secondary anthropogenic sources', 'background mixing ratios'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'The parameterization method based on photochemical aging described in section 2.4 was used to analyze the sources of OVOCs during the daytime (8:00-20:00). Referencing the source analysis results for the OVOCs in Shenzhen from 2014 to 2019 (in Table 2), the measured concentrations of six OVOC species monitored by PTR-MS were in good agreement with the simulated concentrations, and the measured values of each OVOC species were highly correlated with the simulated values (R was between 0.71 and 0.86, except for formic acid, which was 0.62). For the six-year average, the sources of methanol and MEK were similar, with the largest contribution from primary anthropogenic sources  $(47 - 51\\%)$  followed by biogenic and background sources. The contributions to acetaldehyde and acetic acid from primary and secondary anthropogenic sources were similar  $(26 - 32\\%)$  followed by biogenic sources (approximately  $20\\%$ ). In contrast to other OVOCs, acetone was the most affected by regional background concentration  $(37\\%)$  which may be because acetone is relatively inactive and widely exists in the atmosphere, and its ground-level background mixing ratios can reach 500 pptv throughout the troposphere under extremely clean conditions (Seinfeld and Pandis, 2016). For formic acid, secondary anthropogenic sources contributed the most  $(34\\%)$'}. Paper ID: paper_014. Keywords: None

                        Evaluator insight: High-emitting vehicles (top 10% of fleet) disproportionately contribute to total emissions, but their travel behaviors and activity trajectories remain uncharacterized.. Reason: Directly addresses knowledge gaps regarding high emitters' spatial-temporal patterns with concrete methodological proposals for follow-up studies.. Suggested keywords: ['high emitter trajectories', 'vehicle-specific activity matching', 'congestion-aware emission models'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'Due to the limited time span of the vehicle emissions dataset, the interannual variation characteristics of emissions of the high emitters cannot be further analyzed. Future research can fill this gap to explore more suitable cleaning alternative cycles for the high emitters. This study has investigated the contributions of the  $10\\%$  high emitters to the total fleet emissions, but the travel behavior and typical activity trajectories of these high emitters still need to be explored. In the follow-up research, the driving trajectories and hot spots of high emission vehicles can be obtained based on the accurate matching of vehicle-specific emission factors and vehicle-specific activity data, so as to provide more effective support for the managements and controls of target high emission vehicles. The analysis of environmental impacts of traffic congestion reveals the importance of traffic congestion controls on air pollutant reductions. Existing on-road vehicle emission models (i.e., IVE and COPERT (Choudhary and Gokhale, 2019)) usually lack considerations of traffic congestion when calculating on-road emissions, so it can be optimized in future research to better reflect the realistic on-road emissions.'}.                         Evaluator insight: Photochemical OVOC source analysis assumes: 1) proportional tracer-VOC emissions, 2) OH radical scavenging dominance, 3) isoprene-based biogenic scaling, with major uncertainties in tracer validity and emission ratios.. Reason: Details core assumptions and uncertainty drivers of key methodology; critical for evaluating reliability of Chinese OVOC apportionments.. Suggested keywords: ['anthropogenic tracer ratio', 'OH reactivity', 'biogenic emission scaling'].
                        Node content: {'text': 'When using photochemical-age-based parametric methods to analyze the sources of OVOCs, de Gouw et al. (2005) proposed the following assumptions: (1) anthropogenic emissions of OVOCs and their precursors are in direct proportion to the selected tracers of primary emissions; (2) OVOCs are mainly scavenged by reactions with OH radicals during the daytime; and (3) the biogenic source of OVOCs is proportional to the emission of isoprene. Therefore, the uncertainty of the method mainly depends on the effectiveness of the selected tracer, the assumed stable emission ratio and the simplified variability of VOC properties (de Gouw et al., 2005). In this study, some modifications were made to the equation based on the above assumptions to make the model more suitable for the'}. Paper ID: paper_014. Keywords: None

                        Node content: {'text': 'studies (Lu et al., 2021). Previous studies have reported the traffic congestion emissions from a limited number of vehicles and certain roads (i.e., one signalized intersection) (Alobaidi et al., 2020; Choudhary and Gokhale, 2019), but the real-world traffic congestion emissions for the typical urban road networks have rarely been reported. Ko et al. (2019) tested real-world driving emissions of a Euro-6 diesel vehicle and reported that  $\\mathrm{NO}_{\\mathrm{x}}$  emissions under the heavy traffic conditions were  $29\\%$  higher than those under smooth traffic conditions. Alobaidi et al. (2020) revealed a significant impact of the congestion on air pollution at intersections with  $8 - 25\\%$  reductions achieved by optimizing cycle and phase times, and preventing vehicles from stopping near the signalized intersection. The main obstacle towards accurately quantifying traffic congestion emissions is mainly associated with the lack of high temporal and spatial resolution observations of on-road traffic monitoring data and real-time online vehicle emission calculation platforms.'}.
